title: Prevention and Management of Hypothermia (Chapter 10)
learningObjectives: Measure temperature with and without a thermometer.
Classify the degree of hypothermia.
Describe how hypothermia occurs.
Describe how to prevent hypothermia in the neonate.
Describe methods to re‑warm a hypothermic NYI.
Know and understand the equipment used in warming the neonate.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The NYI is most vulnerable to hypothermia during the first few hours after birth, although the condition may occur later too, for example during bathing, on a cold night, or during transportation, if measures to keep the baby warm are inadequate. Sick or low birth weight babies admitted to neonatal units with hypothermia are more likely to die than those admitted with normal temperatures.

subsections.title: Temperature Recording Using a Digital Thermometer
subsections.content:
Axillary temperature: This method is the routine practice for measuring temperature. It has fewer risks than rectal measurements in terms of injury or infection. Point the tip of the thermometer to the shoulder not the bed to avoid the thermometer reading the air temperature. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.

Rectal temperature: Do not use this method for routine monitoring. However, it is the best guide for core temperature in cold (hypothermic) sick neonates. Lubricate the thermometer, insert the tip of the thermometer into the rectum not more than an inch. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.

subsections.title: Assessment of Temperature by Touch
subsections.content: An easy way to assess a newborn baby’s temperature is by “touch”. This can be easily taught to caregivers. The baby’s abdomen is felt with the back of hand and compared with their own forehead. Abdominal temperature represents the core temperature and it is reliable in the diagnosis of hypothermia. Warm and pink feet of the baby indicate that the baby is in thermal comfort. But when feet are cold and trunk is warm, it indicates that the baby has cold stress.

sections.title: Classification of Hypothermia
sections.content:
The NYI has a normal body temperature between 36.5‑37.4 °C.

**Classification table**
- **Mild**: 36.0‑36.4 °C
- **Moderate**: 32.0‑35.9 °C
- **Severe**: < 32 °C

A community‑based study has found that mortality increased by approximately 80 % for every degree Celsius decrease in first observed axillary temperature, and that relative risk of death ranged from 2 to 30 times for moderate hypothermia, increasing with greater severity. Other epidemiological data from India, Malawi and additional studies are summarized in the source text.

subsections.title: Four ways a neonate may lose heat to the environment
subsections.content: Newborn baby’s temperature falls within seconds of being born and sick NYI are often hypothermic. If the temperature continues to fall the baby will become sick and may even die.

table.prevention: Immediately after birth dry baby with a clean, pre‑warmed, dry cloth and then wrap in another dry warm cloth.

table.method: Conduction (e.g., contact with a cold surface of a weighing scale)
table.prevention: Put the baby on the mother’s abdomen for the first 30 min or on a warm surface, delay weighing if room too cold. Place a light, dry material in the weighing scale and zero the scale.
Close the windows, switch off fans and air‑conditioners and, if circumstances permit, wait for the room to warm up to 25 °C before performing a Caesarean section.
Provide a warm, draught‑free room for delivery or neonatal ward; at least 25 °C.

sections.title: The Warm Chain for the Newborn
sections.content: These are procedures to be taken at birth and during the next few hours and days in order to minimize heat loss in all newborns.

subsections.title: 10 steps in the warm chain
list: Warm delivery room – when switching off the air conditioner wait for the room to warm to 25 °C (look at the room thermometer).
Pre‑warm two cloths – use one immediately to dry the baby, discard this wet cloth and wrap baby in the other dry cloth.
Immediate drying of a baby.
Resuscitation – environment must be warm.
Skin‑to‑skin – administer for the first 30‑60 min unless clinical complications; caregiver and baby must be kept together at all times.
Breastfeeding – commence within the first hour unless clinical contraindications.
Clothing – appropriate and warm clothing / bedding should be used.
Transportation – ensure warmth is maintained by protecting the baby from draughts (e.g., KMC position or well wrapped with a hat).
Bathing – delay until at least 24 hours after birth.
Training and awareness – all healthcare professionals and caregivers should be trained so the risks of hypothermia are minimised.

subsections.title: Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate
Skin‑to‑skin Contact (Kangaroo Mother Care)
subsections.content: If there are no signs of distress, a mother can provide a warm environment with skin‑to‑skin contact for the baby. If the baby is < 2500 g this should be continued as KMC. Place the baby, with a nappy and hat, upright inside mother’s clothing against mother’s bare skin over the chest. A loose blouse, sweater, or wrap tied at the waist holds the baby. The baby should wear a hat. Let baby suckle at the breast as often as needed, but at least every 2 hours.

subsections.title: Postpone Bathing
subsections.content: Bathing should be delayed until at least 24 hours after birth. Blood, meconium and some vernix will have been wiped off during drying at birth. The remaining vernix does not need to be removed; it may reduce heat loss and is re‑absorbed through the skin during the first days of life.

subsections.title: Postpone Weighing
subsections.content: Weighing the baby at birth puts him/her at risk of heat loss and should be postponed for at least two hours unless the room temperature is warm.

subsections.title: Appropriate Clothing and Bedding
subsections.content: As a rule, newborns need one or two more layers of clothing and bedding than adults. Covers should not be tight to allow air spaces between the layers as trapped air is a very efficient insulator. Keep ambient atmospheric temperature warm for the baby’s weight and post‑natal age. Monitor body temperature every 4 hours.

subsections.title: Place in a hot‑cot (warmer) when mother cannot stay with the baby
subsections.content: If a baby cannot stay with their mother for KMC, a hot‑cot is helpful. The Blantyre hot‑cot is a simple incubator that uses four 60‑watt light bulbs to raise the air temperature within the cot by 1.5 °C per bulb. A baby may need one, two, three or all four bulbs on to stay warm. Check the baby’s temperature after an hour in the cot; if temperature increases, turn off two bulbs to avoid overheating and re‑check regularly.

sections.title: Signs and Symptoms of Hypothermia
list: Reduced activity / lethargy
Poor feeding
Weak cry
Respiratory distress
Hypoglycaemia

sections.title: Treatment of Established Hypothermia in the NYI
sections.content: The NYI should be re‑warmed carefully and gradually to prevent seizures; the temperature needs to rise by 0.5 °C per hour. The method selected depends on how sick the NYI is and on the availability of mother, staff and equipment.
list: Skin‑to‑skin – ideal method for clinically stable babies; keep the baby with the mother until temperature is normal.
In a hot‑cot – remove cold clothes, replace with pre‑warmed clothes and add a hat. Functional incubators can also be used.
Radiant warmers – usually for short‑term use.

sections.title: Supportive Care
list: Continue breast‑feeding.
If infant too weak, give expressed breast milk by cup, spoon or oro/nasogastric tube.
Assess for infection.
Monitor oxygen saturation and heart rate.
Monitor glucose.
Measure temperature hourly until 36.5 °C.
Watch for apnoea.

sections.title: E 8 Radiant Warmer
sections.content:
Radiant warmers may be used on any neonatal patient admitted to the nursery ward, but especially for:

- Initial assessment of a sick or premature baby
- Hypothermia
- Undertaking invasive procedures
- Resuscitation
- When stabilising a sick baby

See the dedicated Radiant Warmer module for more information.

title: How to Manage Hypoglycaemia or Hyperglycaemia in NYI (Chapter 11)
learningObjectives: Define hypoglycaemia.

sections.title: Overview of Hypoglycaemia in NYI
sections.content: Hypoglycaemia is common in low‑birth‑weight and very sick newborn infants (NYI) and should always be considered early in management. 20 % of infants below seven days of age have hypoglycaemia. It is important to identify hypoglycaemia because it is associated with convulsions, permanent brain injury and increased mortality.

sections.title: Definition
sections.content: COIN defines hypoglycaemia as blood glucose of < 45 mg/dL (2.5 mmol/L) for NYI. Blood glucose levels can be checked using a glucometer.

sections.title: Newborns at Risk of Hypoglycaemia
sections.content: Any baby who has:

sections.title: E 9 Glucometer
sections.content: Assessment of blood glucose with a glucometer should be conducted as part of routine assessment for all infants on admission and during continuing management for all sick or at‑risk patients.
listItems: Failure to feed or poor feeding

sections.title: How to Make Up a 10 % Dextrose Solution When You Only Have 50 % Dextrose
sections.content: Use the following mixing ratios (4 parts water or compatible IV fluid to 1 part 50 % dextrose):

header: Water for Injection / Ringers Lactate / Normal Saline (4 parts)

sections.title: How to Make Up a Bag of 10 % Dextrose Solution
sections.content: Empty fluid from a litre bag of IV normal saline until only 200 ml remain (4 parts) and then add 50 ml of 50 % dextrose (1 part) to make a total of 250 ml of 10 % dextrose solution.

header: 200 ml of normal saline / RL solution

sections.notes: Giving 50 % glucose is no longer recommended because of reported cases of dextrose overdose leading to convulsions, death and possible brain damage.

sections.title: Maintenance Therapy
sections.content: After a bolus of glucose a plan must be made to continue providing a glucose supply as:
listItems: IV fluids with glucose and electrolytes

sections.title: Hyperglycaemia
sections.content: Neonatal hyperglycaemia is defined as a blood glucose of > 145 mg/dL (8 mmol/L) regardless of body weight, gestational or post‑natal age. It is more common in low‑birth‑weight infants and usually results from stress (sepsis, dehydration, HIE) or excessive glucose in IV fluids.

subsections.title: What Should Be Done When a Neonate Has a High Glucose Reading?
orderedList: Repeat the test to confirm accuracy (use a different glucometer if needed).
Perform a full physical examination, monitor vital signs, look for dehydration, fever, acidosis and treat any underlying cause.
If on IV fluids, confirm correct preparation and appropriate infusion rate for age/weight.
If blood glucose > 180 mg/dL (10 mmol/L), discuss with a referral facility for further guidance.
Monitor the baby and blood sugar every 2‑4 hours.

title: Fluid Management in a NYI (Chapter 12)
learningObjectives: Describe how maintenance fluids (feeds) and maintenance fluids (intravenous) are calculated according to weight and age.
List indications for intravenous (IV) fluids.
Describe how to monitor a NYI who is receiving IV fluids.
Describe when and how to introduce oral fluids (feeds).

subsections.title: Exclusive Breastfeeding & General Feeding Principles
subsections.contentSummary: Breastfeeding is the preferred method for most newborn infants (NYI).  If breastfeeding on demand is impossible, expressed breast milk is given.  Maintenance fluid needs replace losses and are calculated on the infant’s age and weight.  Early feeds are small and increased gradually; feeds are given every 2‑3 hours via naso‑/oro‑gastric tube or cup depending on maturity.

subsections.title: Calculation of Feeds for NYI (breast milk)
subsections.contentSummary: Table‑based daily fluid requirements: Day 1 = 60 ml/kg/day, Day 2 = 90 ml/kg/day, Day 3 = 120 ml/kg/day, Day 4 = 150 ml/kg/day, Day 7 & low‑birth‑weight infants can be increased to 180 ml/kg/day once oral feeds are tolerated.

header: How to calculate maintenance fluid requirements
rows: Increase to 180 ml/kg per day after a few days if oral feeds are tolerated

subsections.title: Feeding of Premature Infants
subsections.contentSummary: Infants < 34 weeks gestation may not be able to breastfeed; they receive tube or cup feeds, given in smaller volumes more frequently (ideally every 2 hours, sometimes hourly).  Reference wall‑charts provide volume calculations based on age, maturity and feeding frequency.

subsections.title: Indications / Contra‑indications for Oral Feeding
subsections.contentSummary: IV fluids are indicated when oral feeding is contraindicated (medical or surgical).  Medical reasons include severe/recurrent apnoea, severe respiratory distress, frequent convulsions, unconsciousness.  Surgical reasons include bowel obstruction, necrotising enterocolitis, abdominal wall defects (relative), tracheo‑oesophageal fistula.

rows:
Severe or recurrent apnoea
Severe respiratory distress
Frequent convulsions
Unconsciousness

Bowel obstruction
Necrotising enterocolitis
Abdominal wall defects (relative)
Tracheo‑oesophageal fistula

subsections.title: Which fluid?
subsections.contentSummary: Maintenance fluid replaces losses and is calculated from age and weight; IV fluids should not exceed 100 ml/kg/day to avoid over‑hydration.  Wall‑charts give hourly rates based on weight/age.  When a syringe pump is unavailable, drip rate is calculated using drops per ml of the giving set (20, 15 or 60 drops/ml).

header: How many drops in one ml of fluid?
rows:
1 ml = 20 drops (most common)
1 ml = 15 drops (for blood)
1 ml = 60 drops (paediatric)

Amount of fluid (ml) × drops/ml ÷ time (minutes) = drops/minute
Example: 40 ml/hr with 20‑drop set → (40 × 20)/60 = 13 drops/min

subsections.title: Monitor the IV infusion very carefully
subsections.contentSummary: Use a monitoring sheet, calculate and check drip rate and infused volume each hour.  Clinical monitoring includes heart rate, pulse volume, respiratory rate, skin perfusion, oedema/puffy eyes, daily weight, urine output, and inspection of cannula site.  Weight and urine output are the best overall guides to fluid adequacy.  Oral feeds are introduced when safe, IV rates are reduced accordingly, and IV fluids are discontinued once oral intake reaches ≈ 2/3 of total requirements.

subsections.title: Introducing enteral feeds when the baby has been on IV fluids
subsections.contentSummary: If the infant is stable and there is no contraindication, start enteral feeds at 10 ml/kg/day, check residual volume before next feed, and increase slowly while weaning IV fluids.  If residual > 25 % or abdominal distension, do not increase volume.  Vomiting warrants restarting IV fluids and stopping enteral feeds.  If a contraindication exists, continue IV fluids and restrict fluids if signs of overload appear (weight gain, tachycardia, fast breathing, oedema, puffy eyes, excessive urine output).

header: If stable and feeds are not contraindicated
If there is a contraindication to oral fluids/feeds
rows:
Start enteral feeds 10 ml/kg/day
Measure residual volume before next feed
If residual > 25 % or abdominal distension → do not increase volume
If tolerated → slowly increase feeds and wean IV fluids when oral intake ≈ 2/3 maintenance
If vomiting → restart IV fluids, stop enteral feeds

Continue IV fluids according to age
Do not increase fluids; consider restricting if:
- Weight gain
- Tachycardia
- Fast breathing
- Oedema
- Puffy eyes
- Excessive urine output

title: Jaundice and Phototherapy (Chapter 13)
learningObjectives: Describe physiological and pathological jaundice, including prolonged jaundice.
Describe how to evaluate jaundice including assess its severity.
Describe the management of jaundiced NYI.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.summary: More than 50 % of normal newborns and 80 % of preterm infants become jaundiced. Early mother‑baby contact and frequent breastfeeding reduce the need for treatment.

tables.caption: Comparison of physiological, pathological and prolonged jaundice
rows: Appears after 48 h of life; peaks at 4‑7 days; usually resolves without treatment (some NYI need phototherapy).
Starts on day 1; may be accompanied by fever; deep jaundice involves palms and soles.
Lasts >14 days in term infants and >21 days in preterm infants.
Possible causes: Haemolysis, Neonatal sepsis, Congenital infection
Possible causes: Hypothyroidism, Neonatal hepatitis, Biliary atresia (pale stool, dark urine)

sections.title: Evaluation for Aetiology – History
sections.summary: Key historical clues guide differential diagnosis of neonatal jaundice.

tables.caption: History vs. Possible Diagnosis
rows: Fever, Lethargy, Poor feeding, High‑pitched/reduced cry, Maternal UTI, foul amniotic fluid, maternal fever
Onset < 24 h, Family history of haemolytic disease, Sibling required phototherapy, Failure of phototherapy

sections.title: Evaluation for Aetiology – Examination
sections.summary: Physical examination findings that point toward specific diagnoses.

sections.title: Assessment of Severity of Jaundice
sections.summary: Clinical assessment using blanching, the Kramer scale, and, when available, transcutaneous or serum bilirubin measurements. Also look for signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (kernicterus) and dehydration.

sections.title: Investigations
items: Infection screen (LP, blood culture, urine dipstick)
Liver function tests (transaminases, total & direct bilirubin)

sections.title: Treatment
sections.summary: Primary therapy is phototherapy together with treatment of the underlying cause. Exchange transfusion is reserved for markedly high bilirubin levels.

subsections.title: Phototherapy
subsections.content: Begin when any visible jaundice is present at birth, jaundice extends below the umbilicus, or bilirubin reaches treatment thresholds. Maintain baby naked except for a nappy, cover eyes, turn baby every 2 h, monitor bilirubin daily, ensure adequate feeding, and watch for dehydration.

subsections.title: Exchange Transfusion
subsections.content: Indicated when bilirubin rises above specific cut‑off levels (see Phototherapy chapter for exact values).

sections.title: Prevention of Jaundice
items: Skin‑to‑skin contact immediately after delivery and early breastfeeding (within 1 h).
Room‑in: baby stays with mother 24 h/day and feeds on demand.
If separation is unavoidable, provide expressed breast milk every three hours.

sections.title: Phototherapy Lights (E 11)
sections.summary: Indications for initiating phototherapy and practical management while the baby is under lights.

subsections.title: While on Phototherapy
items: Baby naked except for nappy; eyes covered with gauze.
Ensure adequate feeding – supplement with expressed breast milk via cup or NGT if needed.

sections.title: Prolonged Jaundice
sections.summary: Jaundice persisting >14 days in term infants or >21 days in preterm infants is abnormal and may indicate infection, hypothyroidism, hepatitis, or biliary atresia. Pale stools or dark urine warrant referral for ultrasound, thyroid function tests, and direct/indirect bilirubin measurement.

title: Shock in NYI (Chapter 14)
learningObjectives: Define shock in a NYI.
Assess NYI for shock.
Describe the steps in the management of shock in a NYI.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The letters C in “ABCCCD” stand for Circulation, Coma and Convulsions. This module will help with the systematic assessment, resuscitation and treatment of all NYI with life‑threatening conditions that are most frequently seen in infants less than 2 months of age.

sections.title: Definition
sections.content: Shock, also called ‘severe impaired circulation’, can be defined as the life‑threatening failure of adequate oxygen delivery to the tissues and may be due to decreased blood perfusion of tissues, inadequate blood oxygen saturation, or increased oxygen demand from the tissues that results in decreased end‑organ oxygenation and dysfunction. If left untreated, shock results in sustained multiple organ dysfunction, end‑organ damage and possible death.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content: All sick infants are assessed for Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Coma, Convulsions and severe Dehydration (ABCCCD). Efforts should be made to maintain normal blood glucose and a normal body temperature while managing ABCCCD.

subsections.title: Assess Circulation for Signs of Shock
subsections.content: Rapid assessment of circulation after airway and breathing. Signs include: baby alert?, hands cold?, temperature gradient up limbs?, capillary refill time (CRT) > 3 s?, pulse fast and weak?. Shock is present when 3 or more signs are noted.

subsections.title: Additional Findings
subsections.content: Look for dehydration (sunken eyes/decreased skin turgor), pallor, severe wasting/oedema, and assess oxygen saturation, heart rate and blood pressure.

subsections.title: Capillary Refill Time (CRT)
subsections.content: Capillary refill is assessed by applying finger pressure to the centre of the chest over the sternum for 3 seconds. The time from release of pressure to full return of pink colour should be < 3 seconds. Longer times suggest shock, unless the environment is cold.

subsections.title: Pulse Assessment
subsections.content: A strong, non‑fast radial pulse (≤ 160 bpm) is adequate. Absence of a radial pulse or a weak/faster pulse requires checking the femoral pulse and assessing hydration status.

sections.title: Treatment of Shock
sections.content: Treatment of shock requires teamwork. The following actions need to be started simultaneously, beginning with fluid administration for shock or impaired circulation.

images.alt: Overview chart for shock (assessment and treatment)

title: Sepsis in NYI (Chapter 15)
learningObjectives: Describe the risks for sepsis in a NYI.
Describe how a NYI may present with sepsis.
Describe the management of sepsis in a NYI (drug dosages, supportive care and duration of antibiotic treatment).

sections.title: Risk factors for Sepsis
sections.content:
NYI have immature immune systems and have just been colonised with bacteria during their recent delivery. They are therefore prone to infections which are likely to cross barriers (lungs‑blood, gut‑blood, meninges). Many NYI infections can be prevented by good hygiene at birth, early and exclusive breastfeeding, appropriate umbilical cord care, appropriate eye care, using Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) and avoiding separation of the mother and infant.

Common systemic bacterial infections in young infants include sepsis, pneumonia, urinary tract infection and meningitis and all these may present alike. Sepsis is a clinical syndrome of systemic illness accompanied by septicaemia (bacteria in the blood). It is also called bacteraemia.

**Risk Factors for Sepsis and Clues to Infection**

- Intrapartum maternal fever (temperature > 38 °C)
- Membranes ruptured > 18 h before delivery
- Foul‑smelling or purulent amniotic fluid
- Untreated STI such as syphilis
- Suspected or confirmed sepsis in a twin

Babies born to mothers with these risk factors may be symptomatic or asymptomatic. Both groups should be treated as having sepsis with IV antibiotics because the risk of neonatal infection and mortality rates are high.

sections.title: Initial Assessment and Treatment of Sepsis in the NYI
sections.content:
ABCCCD – Assess ABCD and manage the problems identified. Provide supportive care and monitoring for the sick NYI.

Start empiric antibiotics; give penicillin and gentamicin – see doses in wall‑charts. Give (flu)cloxacillin instead of penicillin if extensive skin pustules or abscesses are present as these may indicate a staphylococcal infection.

sections.title: Symptoms and Signs of sepsis in a NYI
sections.content: Typical clinical features are:
listItems: Axillary temperature ≥ 37.5 °C or ≤ 35.5 °C (or feels hot to touch)
Umbilical redness extending to the periumbilical skin or umbilicus draining pus, skin pustules, big boil (abscess)
Painful joint/swelling, reduced movement and irritability when the part is handled
Grunting, nasal flaring, fast breathing, chest in‑drawing, crepitations in the lungs

sections.title: Investigations
sections.content: A sepsis screen should be done before IV antibiotics. The screen includes:
listItems: White blood cell count (< 5 000 or > 20 000 cells/µl are very likely sepsis in age > 72 h)

sections.title: Empiric antibiotic therapy and dose for sepsis
sections.content:
If a baby develops a fever after > 72 h in a neonatal unit it is likely a hospital‑acquired infection; add ceftriaxone. If < 72 h, benzyl‑penicillin + gentamicin are the drugs of choice.

**Dosage tables**

tables.caption: First‑line antibiotics
rows: LBW: 3 mg/kg; Term: 5 mg/kg; > 7 days: 7.5 mg/kg

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers: Supportive Care for NYI with Sepsis
listItems: Gentle stimulation if apnoeic; consider aminophylline if premature and estimated gestational age < 37 weeks.
Respiratory support with oxygen or CPAP if severe respiratory distress or apnoea.
If shocked, treat according to the impaired circulation protocol.
If hypoglycaemic, give 2 ml/kg of 10 % dextrose stat and re‑check in 30 min; continue maintenance 10 % dextrose.
If Vitamin K not given, give 1 mg intramuscularly (septic NYI have increased bleeding tendency).
If very sick (e.g., continuous convulsions), avoid oral feeds, give maintenance IV fluids, treat convulsions if present.
Treat jaundice if present with phototherapy.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers: Empiric Antibiotics and Duration
listItems: Empiric antibiotics are chosen before the causative organism is identified; they cover the most likely pathogens for this age group.
If there is no blood culture or the culture is negative and the baby is well, continue empiric antibiotics for a minimum of 5 days.
If the baby was clinically septic, treat for 7‑10 days (longer for meningitis or bone/joint infection).
If no improvement after 48 h, consider changing from first‑line (penicillin + gentamicin) to second‑line (often ceftriaxone, depending on local policy).

sections.title: How to Prevent Sepsis in a NYI
listItems: Use Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) for premature babies.
Maintain good hand hygiene for staff, visitors and caregivers.
Screen mother for HIV and VDRL; treat neonate when necessary.
Keep baby normothermic at all times.
Remove IV cannulas as quickly as possible.

title: Coma and Convulsions (Chapter 16)
learningObjectives: Describe the assessment (AVPU) of a NYI in a coma.
Describe the management of a NYI in a coma.
Describe the assessment of a NYI with a convulsion.
Describe the step‑wise management of a NYI with a convulsion.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers – How to Assess the NYI for Coma
sections.content: The AVPU scale is used to assess a newborn/infant’s level of consciousness.

subsections.title: AVPU Scale
items: U – Unresponsive to voice/shake and pain → Unconscious

sections.title: How to Assess for Convulsions
sections.content: Assessment is based on observation of the child and history from the parent. Seizures in neonates may be subtle (facial twitching, apnea, lip‑smacking, eye/hand/foot movements).

table.Tetanus: Increases with tactile stimulation, wind, light and noise
table.Convulsions: Does not change in response to stimulation

sections.title: Management of Coma and Convulsion
sections.content: Treatment of coma and convulsion is similar and is described together.

sections.title: Management of Convulsion
sections.content: Airway management: gentle suction, place infant on side, start oxygen, do NOT insert objects into mouth.

subsections.title: Infant from birth up to 2 weeks of age with seizures
subsections.note: Phenobarbitone preparation assumed 200 mg/ml; dose is 20 mg/kg.

subsections.title: Managing seizures beyond 2 weeks of age
subsections.note: Phenobarbitone preparation assumed 200 mg/ml; dose is 20 mg/kg.

subsections.title: Loading Dose of Phenobarbitone for Young Infants
subsections.content: Phenobarbitone IV dose 20 mg/kg (200 mg/ml).
subsections.note: Phenobarbitone is usually given IM, not IV.

subsections.title: Dosage of Diazepam
list: Given rectally using a 10 mg/2 ml solution.
subsections.note: Do NOT give in babies < 2 weeks – may cause respiratory arrest.

subsections.title: Paraldehyde Dosage
list: Preferably given rectally.
subsections.note: Do not leave in plastic syringe > 10‑15 min; never give IV.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers – How to Assess and Manage the NYI for Coma and Convulsions
list: Assess conscious level using AVPU scale
Manage airway, breathing and circulation
Always check blood glucose and correct hypoglycaemia
Give appropriate anticonvulsants when indicated
Follow step‑wise initial management table (airway, positioning, glucose, drugs)

imageLinks.description: Algorithm for managing convulsions – Infant up to 2 weeks
Algorithm for managing convulsions – Infant beyond 2 weeks

title: Meningitis in NYI (Chapter 17)
learningObjectives: Define meningitis.
Describe how a NYI with meningitis may present.
Describe the management of a NYI with meningitis.

sections.title: Definition of Meningitis
content: Meningitis is inflammation of the meninges.

sections.title: Symptoms and Signs
content: Suspect meningitis in an infant with sepsis or if they present with any of the following clinical symptoms or signs:
Note: NYI often do not have neck stiffness.

imageLinks.description: Illustration of a young infant with possible meningitis signs

sections.title: Investigations
content: To confirm the diagnosis of meningitis, a lumbar puncture must be done immediately unless the young infant is convulsing actively or is unstable.
Also obtain a blood culture, urine dip‑stick, microscopy and culture (if available) or a urine analysis.

sections.title: Empiric Therapy for Meningitis
content: How to calculate the dose – see wall‑charts for detailed dosing.

tables.caption: Empiric antibiotic regimen (dose per kilogram) and frequency
rows: LBW 3 mg/kg/dose; Term 5 mg/kg/dose for 1st week; > 7 days 7.5 mg/kg/dose

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers – Supportive Care for NYI with Meningitis

items: Respiratory support with oxygen or CPAP if there is severe respiratory distress or apnoea.
Gentle stimulation if apnoeic; consider aminophylline if premature and estimated gestational age <37 weeks.
If shocked, treat according to the impaired circulation protocol.
If hypoglycaemic, give 2 ml/kg of 10 % dextrose stat and re‑check in 30 min; continue maintenance 10 % dextrose.
Treat jaundice if present with phototherapy.
If very sick (e.g., continuous convulsions) avoid oral feeds and give maintenance fluids.
Give Vitamin K – 1 mg IM to term baby or 0.5 mg IM to premature baby if not already administered.
Reassess therapy based on culture and antibiotic‑sensitivity results if possible.
Continue IV antibiotics for at least 2 weeks for GBS or 3 weeks for Gram‑negative bacteria.
If the organism is unknown, treat for 3 weeks to cover possible Gram‑negative pathogens.
Measure head circumference every 3 days; if increasing, obtain a cranial ultrasound to assess for intracranial abscess or hydrocephalus.

imageLinks.description: Illustration associated with the Symptoms and Signs section

referenceLinks.text: Wall charts – Antibiotic and Aminophylline doses

title: Diarrhoea and Dehydration in NYI (Chapter 18)
learningObjectives: Assess the severity of dehydration.
Describe the management of no, moderate and severe dehydration.

sections.title: Assessment of Severe Dehydration
sections.content: In triage the letter D stands for Dehydration.  This section explains how to assess the degree of dehydration in infants with diarrhoea, vomiting or poor feeding, differentiating normal breast‑feeding stools from true diarrhoea.

subsections.title: History taking
subsections.content: Ask about duration of diarrhoea/vomiting and whether blood is present in the stool.

subsections.title: Clinical signs of severe dehydration
subsections.content: Key observations: lethargy, sunken eyes, prolonged skin‑pinch (> 2 s), inability to suck or drink.

sections.title: Assessment of the Degree of Dehydration (Table)
sections.content: Comparison of clinical findings and corresponding WHO management plans (A, B, C).

examination: At least 2 of: unable to drink, sunken eyes, skin‑pinch > 2 s
management: Plan C (see Treatment of Severe Dehydration)
If sepsis/low weight → start antibiotics, admit or refer

examination: At least 2 of: restless/irritable, thirsty/drinks eagerly, sunken eyes, skin‑pinch > 2 s
management: Plan B (see Treatment of Some Dehydration)
If sepsis/low weight → start antibiotics, admit or refer
Plan A (see Treatment of No Dehydration)
Home care, educate mother, follow‑up in 3 days

sections.title: Treatment of Severe Dehydration – Plan C
sections.content: Use a syringe pump if available.  Give 100 ml/kg Ringer’s lactate IV (or normal saline if RL unavailable).  If the child can drink, give ORS by mouth/NG.  Reassess hourly and provide supportive care.

subsections.title: IV Fluid Regimen
subsections.content: First 30 ml/kg over 1 hour (0‑2 months), then 70 ml/kg over 5 hours.

subsections.title: ORS Alternative if IV not possible
subsections.content: Give ORS 20 ml/kg/hour for 6 hours (120 ml/kg) via NG tube.

subsections.title: Supportive Care
subsections.content: Keep infant warm, O₂ sat > 90 %, check blood glucose (treat if < 2.5 mmol/L), treat jaundice if present.

sections.title: Treatment of Some Dehydration – Plan B
sections.content: Determine ORS volume for the first 4 hours (≈ weight × 75 ml).  Provide instructions for frequent small sips, continued breastfeeding, and warning signs.

tables.title: Volume of ORS – Plan B for the NYI
headers: Amount of ORS (per hour for 4 hours)

sections.title: After 4 Hours – Re‑assessment
sections.content: Re‑evaluate the child, classify dehydration again and move to the appropriate plan (A, B, or C).  Teach the mother to prepare ORS at home, give 5 ml/kg after each loose stool, and administer zinc 10 mg daily for 10‑14 days if < 6 months.

sections.title: Treatment of No Dehydration – Plan A
sections.content: Explain home‑care rules: give extra fluid as tolerated, continue frequent breastfeeding, and educate on red‑flag signs that require return to the hospital.

sections.title: Role of Antibiotics
sections.content: Antibiotics are rarely needed; only give for visibly bloody diarrhoea (after excluding surgical causes).  If needed: Ciprofloxacin 7.5 mg/kg for 3 days or Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV for 5 days.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers
sections.content: Summary checklist for clinicians.
list: Know normal stool patterns for each age group.
Take a focused history.
Assess and classify dehydration.
Apply WHO management plans: A (no), B (some), C (severe).
Antibiotics are seldom required; assess carefully before prescribing.

title: Referral, Transport and Discharge
description: Overview of the processes for referring, transporting, and discharging patients.

sections.title: Referral and Transport of the Sick NYI
learningObjectives: Identify the indications for referring a sick Newborn/Infant (NYI) to a higher level of care.
Describe the steps involved in safely transporting a NYI, including equipment, personnel, and documentation requirements.
Explain communication protocols between referring and receiving facilities to ensure continuity of care.

sections.title: Discharge from the Hospital
learningObjectives: Outline the criteria for medically fit discharge of a patient from the hospital.
Detail the components of a comprehensive discharge plan, including medication reconciliation, follow‑up appointments, and patient education.
Recognize common barriers to safe discharge and strategies to mitigate them.

title: Referral and Transport of the Sick NYI (Chapter 19)
learningObjectives: List conditions that need urgent referral
List conditions that need less urgent referral
Stabilise the sick neonate before referral

sections.title: Introduction
content: All facilities, which do not have capacity to care for the neonate, need to refer to next level of care. Babies requiring next level care should be referred urgently. If the baby needs to be transferred to a special care neonatal unit, ensure a safe and timely transfer. It is important to prepare the baby for transfer, communicate with the receiving facility, and provide care during transfer.

sections.title: Indications of transfer to next level of care
items: Abdominal distension with bilious vomiting and other surgical emergencies.
Major congenital malformations, such as tracheo‑oesophageal fistula, diaphragmatic hernia, meningomyelocele, gastroschisis.
All conditions requiring special care from facilities without a Sick Newborn Care Unit (SNCU).
Facility does not have equipment required for continued care of babies who need next level care.

subsections.title: Step 1: Assess
content: Determine the indication for transporting the baby to a higher health facility.
items: Make a careful assessment of the baby.
Make sure that there is a genuine indication for referral.
Always try to discuss with the referral centre, as in some cases there may be no benefit from transferring.
Major congenital malformations: Infant may require urgent referral (e.g., gastrointestinal atresia, imperforate anus) or less urgent referral (e.g., meningomyelocele, spina bifida).
Abdominal distension with bilious vomiting requires urgent referral.

subsections.title: Step 2: Preparation of baby
items: Stabilise the baby (including temperature, airway, breathing, circulation and blood sugar).
Ensure baby is kept warm with kangaroo care or warmly dressed and covered.
Provide oxygen if indicated (tank of oxygen or portable concentrator, if available).
Secure IV line if necessary and give treatment before transfer, including the first dose of antibiotics.
Continue monitoring and management while awaiting transport.

subsections.title: Step 3: Prepare for transport, including writing a referral note
items: Counsel the parents and family before transport.
Arrange a capable healthcare provider, the mother, and a relative to accompany (if available).
Ensure the mother comes along with the baby, if possible.
Communicate with and write a note to the referral hospital, explaining the need for referral.
If possible, speak directly with the referral facility about the patient’s condition and the decision to refer – this helps them prepare and may avoid unnecessary referrals.
Write a precise note for the receiving providers covering the baby’s condition, pregnancy, labour and delivery history, investigation results, reasons for referral, and treatment given.
Send ALL X‑rays and investigation results with the infant.
Assemble supplies and equipment needed for transport.
Bring extra drugs for the journey (e.g., anticonvulsants, IV fluids, IV glucose).

subsections.title: Step 4: Care of neonate during transport
items: The neonate must be escorted by a competent health worker with equipment to manage emergencies.
Keep the baby warm during transfer – KMC (mother/father/guardian) or proper wrapping, or a transport incubator if available.
Ensure neonate receives feeds or fluids.
Monitor frequently (temperature, airway, breathing, circulation, IV line, infusion).
Stop the ambulance/vehicle to manage problems if necessary.

subsections.title: Step 5: Feedback by tertiary hospital
items: Communicate with the referring team and inform them of:

sublist: Outcome of the baby, post‑discharge advice, and follow‑up.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers: Referral and Transport of Sick Neonate
items: Be clear about indications for transfer to another facility.
Communicate with the facility you are referring to for pre‑referral care and so that they can prepare for the patient.
Prepare the patient – ensure they are stable, kept warm during the transfer, provide appropriate pre‑referral treatment.
Write a clear referral note and send relevant documentation including tests and interventions done.
Ensure transport is available and appropriate.
Ensure ongoing care for the patient as they await transport.
Follow up the patient for feedback.

title: Triage (Chapter 1)
learningObjectives: Define triage
Describe how to triage
Understand ABCCCD and priority concept
Demonstrate triage skills

sections.title: ‘Triage’ means ‘sorting’
sections.intro: Triage is the process of rapidly assessing all sick children when they first arrive in hospital and sorting them according to their need.
keyPoints: Triage assessment can be done anywhere such as in out‑patients or the ward.
Triage should be carried out, on arrival, to all new patients to the hospital.
Triage can be done by any trained person – e.g. a health worker, a receptionist, a guard.

categories.name: EMERGENCIES
categories.label: E
categories.description: Those with life‑threatening illness must be seen immediately.

categories.name: PRIORITIES
categories.label: P
categories.description: Those who are less sick must be seen next.

categories.name: NON‑URGENT (QUEUE)
categories.label: Q
categories.description: Those who are non‑urgent can wait their turn in the queue.

categoryTable.definition: patient must be seen at once; may need life‑saving treatment
patient needs rapid assessment; needs to be seen soon
patient can safely wait to be seen; *NYI are never in this category

sections.title: EMERGENCIES
sections.description: Emergencies are sent straight to the best place for resuscitation.
sections.abccccdConcept: The ABCCCD concept is used to identify emergencies. It is a quick screening tool, not a replacement for a full examination.

treatments: If present, remove foreign body, BMV
No Malnutrition – Give IV fluids + NGT
Severe Malnutrition – Give NGT, try to avoid IV

sections.title: PRIORITIES
sections.description: Priorities are sent to the front of the queue to be seen quickly. After emergencies are excluded, priority signs are looked for.
sections.mnemonic: 3TPR, MOB – and all infants < 2 months are automatically priorities.

3Ts: Temperature (high temperature as judged by your hand)

title: Discharge from the Hospital (Chapter 20)
learningObjectives: Describe the indications for the discharge of a NYI from hospital.
Describe the elements of the discharge of a NYI from hospital.
List discharge planning advice for family.
introParagraph: Planning of discharge from the hospital is as important as diagnosis and treatment. The NYI has to remain in hospital while receiving oxygen for respiratory instability, IV treatment, if feeding has not been established, or body temperature cannot be maintained.

sections.title: Indications for Discharge
points: Patient stable and has improved.
Baby gaining weight for three (3) consecutive days.
Established lactation in a mother.
Ability to breastfeed or cup feed satisfactorily.

subsections.title: Counsel the mother on:
points: Diagnosis and care given.
Treatment if ongoing and how to administer it.
Exclusive breastfeeding of the infant at home for six (6) months.
Importance of keeping the NYI warm including KMC if < 2000 g (reference 3).
Danger signs and when to present immediately to the nearest healthcare facility.
Upcoming vaccinations.
Importance of attending any follow‑up appointments (e.g., KMC, physiotherapy, audiology, ophthalmology and surgical).

sections.title: Providing Follow‑Up Care
points: Infants who are discharged from the hospital should return for follow‑up in relation to the present problem as needed.
Plan the routine follow‑up with the local provider depending on weight, age, and HIV exposure status of the NYI – clearly write the purpose of the follow‑up (e.g., to do HIV DNA PCR).
Discuss where and when the NYI will receive their next scheduled immunisation.
Discuss where the mother will access care if the NYI shows a danger sign.

sections.title: Record Keeping
points: Ensure the infant’s immunisation status and record is up‑to‑date.
Ensure the HIV exposure status is recorded.
Communicate with the health personnel who referred the infant or who will be responsible for follow‑up care.

Clearly document in the health passport: date of birth, birth and discharge weight, mode of delivery, APGAR scores, date of admission, date of discharge, diagnosis, most recent investigations done, treatment given, ongoing treatment, counselling given and reason for follow‑up.
Documentation must be signed together with a printed name and the health facility for follow‑up should be clear.
If referred from another health facility, complete the feedback form.
Ask the mother to attend the local facility as soon as possible and to show the passport / feedback form to the local healthcare team.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers
points: Know the indications for discharge and ensure that the patient qualifies:
Patient stable and has improved.
Baby gaining weight for three (3) consecutive days.
Established lactation in a mother.
Ability to breastfeed or cup feed satisfactorily.
Counsel mother on diagnosis, follow‑up plan, feeding, and immunisation.
Document clearly what has been done, results of tests and follow‑up plan.

title: Essential Newborn and Young Infant Equipment (Chapter 21)

sections.title: Learning Objectives
sections.description: After completion of this chapter the participant should be able to:
objectives: Describe the minimum equipment required to provide NYI resuscitation.
Assemble the minimum equipment required to provide NYI resuscitation.

sections.title: Equipment Overview
sections.description: This equipment should be available in an area in the delivery suite and neonatal care unit for facilitating immediate care of the newborn and ongoing emergency care. It must be checked regularly and supplies replaced after use.

subsections.title: Equipment
items: Radiant warmer with bassinet (link: E8_Radiant Warmer.html)
Oxygen concentrator (link: E3_Oxygen concentrator.html) + Oxygen flow splitter (link: E5_Oxygen splitter.html)
Self‑inflating resuscitation bag (250 ml‑500 ml) with masks (size 0 and 1)

subsections.title: Supplies
items: Extension tubing to deliver fluid and giving sets

subsections.title: Drugs
items: Oral Nevirapine for HIV‑exposed infants (link: Formulary.html#nevirapine)
Vitamin K – 1 mg for term, 0.5 mg for preterm (link: Formulary.html#vitk)

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers
sections.description: Essential Newborn and Young Infant Equipment and Medication
bulletPoints: Health workers should be aware of the essential equipment needed to run a newborn or young infant unit.
The health workers are responsible for maintaining and cleaning these items on the ward to optimise lifespan and prevent cross infections.
Stock levels and functionality should be monitored and appropriate action taken to ensure their availability.

title: Quality Improvement and Death Audits (Chapter 22)
learningObjectives: Understand what clinical audit is and how it can be conducted.
Understand how death audit can be used as a tool for quality improvement.
Be able to plan and conduct a neonatal death audit as part of a quality improvement process.
Be able to do a root cause analysis using the 5‑why analysis approach and develop action plans.

subsections.title: Steps in Preparing to Conduct a Clinical Audit
subsections.content: Guidance on selecting a topic, defining the audit period, obtaining data, ethics, and keeping the audit simple.

subsections.title: Conducting the Clinical Audit
subsections.content: How to extract data, populate a data‑collection tool, analyse against standards, share results, identify gaps, and plan remedial actions.

subsections.title: Audit Data Collection Tool (Blood‑Glucose Example)
subsections.content: Table showing fields such as date of birth, sex, admission time, glucose test Y/N, and comments.

subsections.title: Monitoring Progress
subsections.content: Use of repeat audits, target vs. achieved tables, and visual display of progress on a graph.

subsections.title: How to Conduct a Neonatal Death Audit
subsections.content: Overview of the audit cycle, purpose (no‑blame), and the importance of routine, ongoing reviews.

subsections.title: Team Composition & Planning
subsections.content: Suggested participants (administrator, clinicians, QIST members, data clerk, etc.) and steps for preparing the review form, source documents, and meeting logistics.

subsections.title: Conducting the Death Audit
subsections.content: Key meeting activities: review minutes, admission/mortality trends, case reviews, cause‑of‑death determination, and identification of modifiable factors.

subsections.title: 5‑Why Analysis
subsections.content: Root‑cause technique that asks “why?” up to five times; example provided for inadequate glucose monitoring.

subsections.title: Summary & Reporting
subsections.content: Compiling findings into the consolidation form, circulating minutes, assigning action‑point owners, and scheduling the next audit.

subsections.title: Key Facts for Providers (Clinical & Death Audits)
subsections.content: Bullet‑point reminder that audits target systems, not people; emphasise a no‑blame approach and multidisciplinary involvement.

title: Good Clinical Practice (Chapter 23)
learningObjectives: Know and demonstrate the key features for good communication in clinical practice.

sections.title: Documentation is really important
content: It is a way of communicating with our colleagues, remembering important events and monitoring progress. Good documentation prevents errors and protects both the patient and the staff.

It is essential that the mother and baby are treated as one unit. The mother needs to know what is happening to her baby and how she can help. We must make her feel welcome, important and central to the well‑being of her baby.
The health workers are a team. We need to communicate with each other, respect and value each other and include the mother in the care of her baby. She is an extended part of the team.

The death of a baby is devastating to the mother and the baby’s family. We must be sensitive to their concern and grief, respectful of religious and cultural beliefs and be prepared to express our sorrow at their loss. Medical diagnoses and treatment given need to be explained to the family. It is important for the family to know that their loss is through no fault of the mother and she should not be blamed.

subsections.title: Who do we need to communicate with?
items: Colleagues – seniors/juniors; nurses and doctors, other health staff.
Parents/guardians – explanations or breaking bad news.

subsections.title: When do we need to communicate?
Consequences of poor communication
subsections.content: Poor communication leads to mistakes and to mismanagement. Poor clinical handover can result in:

sections.title: Communication Styles
content: Learn to listen; try to understand why and what is being asked. Is your body language welcoming and interested? Don’t stand over a person you are talking to.

steps.title: Is the setting right for this discussion?
steps.description: The corridor is not the place for discussions. Remember confidentiality! Speak with parents in quiet privacy. Never tell someone off in public.

steps.title: Is the timing right?
steps.description: If calling for help, do it early rather than too late. If speaking to family, talk to the right members and take time. If a reprimand is needed – don’t delay.

steps.title: Have you got all the data you need?
steps.description: Do you know the full story? If it is a dispute have you heard both sides? Listen first! If you are reporting about a patient, check the notes, get all the facts.

steps.title: Using a standardised way of communicating
steps.description: It helps to present a clinical problem in a standardised way. Many people use the letters ISBAR to remember to say everything.

steps.title: Training helps
steps.description: It helps to practice. We often get into bad habits that we are unaware of. A training chapter is a good place to learn good habits and get rid of bad ones.

format.label: Identity
format.description: Give your name and your position (e.g., This is Mary Phiri, sister in charge of the neonatal ward).

format.label: Situation
format.description: Give the patient’s name, diagnosis and what the problem is now.

format.label: Background
format.description: Give a brief history of the patient and what drugs they are on.

format.label: Assessment
format.description: Say what you have found e.g., Temp, SaO2, RR, PR, and why you are concerned.

format.label: Recommendations
format.description: Say what you want the person that you phoned to do (e.g., come to the ward urgently), repeat what has been said to you so that you are sure it is correct.

sections.title: Monitoring in Health Care
content: Anticipating clinical deterioration and possibly stopping it from happening makes good sense. To do this monitoring is critical; it helps us decide when to act to prevent problems and when to call for help.
Nurses are with the patients all the time and measuring, monitoring, and assessing progress is one of their very important tasks.

subsections.title: Vital sign monitoring
subsections.description: Includes heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2, temperature, blood pressure and conscious level. In different circumstances additional monitoring is needed (e.g., fluid input/output for IVs, pupil size for head injuries).

subsections.title: Other clinical observations
subsections.description: Assess if the child is sucking/drinking, has diarrhoea or vomiting; check breathing pattern, chest indrawing or acidotic breathing.

subsections.title: Pain assessment
subsections.description: Determine if the child is miserable, in pain or frightened to move. Use validated pain scores.

subsections.title: Documentation and interpretation
subsections.description: All findings must be recorded on a specially designed form, not just vital signs. Interpretation is essential (e.g., high temperature, abnormal breathing, AVPU score).

subsections.title: Wall charts
subsections.description: Display normal ranges of vital signs for different ages to aid interpretation.

subsections.title: Early warning/Track and Trigger systems
steps: A score to indicate the seriousness of the sign.
The score leads to an action, such as increasing monitoring frequency or calling a senior.
Rapid response to that call.
Appropriate action must be taken.

subsections.title: Equipment care
subsections.description: Ensure equipment is clean, functional, serviced/calibrated regularly and stored accessibly.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers: Good Clinical Practice
items: Communication is a critical part of providing good clinical care.
Documentation is critical for good communication between colleagues.
Mother and baby are one unit; mother’s involvement is central for the baby’s wellbeing.
Health workers should function as a team, recognising every individual’s contribution and maintaining respect and good communication.
Communication requires clarity about who should communicate, when and how.
Poor communication leads to mistakes and mismanagement.
Monitoring is important as it helps decide when to act to prevent problems and when to call for help.
Monitor vital signs, pain, document and interpret all information collected to make appropriate interventions.

title: Infection Prevention (Chapter 24)
learningObjectives: Understand the infection prevention principles.
Know how to minimise risk of infection within their neonatal unit.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Introduction of essential devices to newborn care units is critical to improving newborn survival.  Devices can increase hospital‑acquired infections if adequate disinfection and cleaning measures are not put into place.  This module focuses on infection‑control measures associated specifically with essential devices, staff and visitors.  For comprehensive guidance see local and WHO guidelines.

sections.title: Ward Infection Prevention
sections.content: Hospitals, wards, equipment and staff are all sources of infection for a baby.  Hospital‑acquired infections (HAI) are often caused by multidrug‑resistant bacteria.  Prevention strategies include hand hygiene, environmental cleanliness, medical‑equipment maintenance and waste disposal.  Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) are key elements.

subsections.title: Handwashing
subsections.content: Hand hygiene is the single most important measure to reduce transmission of infections.  All HCWs, guardians and visitors should wash hands and forearms up to the elbow with soap and water on entry, dry with single‑use towels, and re‑wash whenever visibly soiled.  Alcohol‑based hand‑rub is acceptable when hands are not visibly dirty.  Hand hygiene should be the last action before patient contact and the first action after.

subsections.title: Gloves
subsections.content: Gloves protect both HCWs and patients but do not replace hand hygiene.  Wear gloves only when necessary (e.g., contact with bodily fluids, invasive procedures, contaminated surfaces) and discard immediately after use.  Perform hand washing or sanitising promptly after glove removal.

subsections.title: Dress Code
subsections.content: Staff should wear clean clothing with bare arms below the elbow.  No jewellery (except plain wedding bands) and nails must be short, natural and unvarnished.  Gowns, when used, must not be shared or reused until re‑washed.

subsections.title: Health Screening
subsections.content: Any HCW or guardian with an acute or transmissible infection must not be on the ward.  Guardians with acute respiratory illness should wear a mask and practise meticulous hand washing.

subsections.title: Patient Isolation
subsections.content: Isolation policies should cohort at‑risk patients with similar infections.  Strict hand hygiene is required on entry and exit.  Equipment used in isolation areas must be cleaned and disinfected before returning to the main ward.

subsections.title: Equipment Cleaning
subsections.content: All neonatal equipment (suctions, CPAP, monitors, warmers, etc.) must be cleaned according to manufacturer instructions.  WHO recommends 0.5 % sodium hypochlorite for surfaces contaminated with blood/body fluids; 70 % alcohol for metal and rubber surfaces.

rows.recommendedUse: General disinfectant; kills bacteria, fungi, mycobacteria, spores & viruses; effective in hard‑water.
rows.precautions: Use in well‑ventilated area; respiratory and skin irritant; wear PPE; do NOT mix with acids or ammonia (chlorine gas risk).

rows.recommendedUse: Smooth surfaces, tabletops, aprons, metal/rubber parts where bleach cannot be used; leaves no residue.
rows.precautions: Use in well‑ventilated area; keep away from heat sources, flames, electricity; allow to dry completely before use (fire hazard).

rows.recommendedUse: General disinfectant for surfaces/equipment contaminated with blood & body fluids; kills bacteria, fungi and some enveloped viruses; persistent activity when undisturbed.
rows.precautions: Use correct dilution; do NOT kill spores, TB or non‑enveloped viruses; efficacy reduced by hard water, cotton/gauze, organic matter.

rows.recommendedUse: Primarily an antiseptic rather than a disinfectant.
General disinfectant for surfaces/equipment contaminated with blood & body fluids; unaffected by organic matter; non‑corrosive and safe for workers.
rows.precautions: Higher cost, especially for large‑volume purchases.

rows.recommendedUse: NOT recommended for neonatal wards – cheaper, safer alternatives available.
rows.precautions: May cause hyperbilirubinemia and/or neurotoxicity in neonates.

rows.dilution: 8.5 g powder : 1 L clean water (Ratio [8.5 g : 1 L])
6 tablets : 1 L clean water (Ratio [6 tablets : 1 L])

subsections.title: Routine Cleaning
subsections.content: All horizontal surfaces (bed rails, tables, trolleys, commodes, taps, scales, etc.) must be cleaned and disinfected with an approved detergent or 0.5 % chlorine/70 % alcohol solution at least daily and whenever visibly soiled.  Between patient admissions, cots, incubators and patient beds must be cleaned thoroughly.  Floors, handles and other surfaces are cleaned daily per departmental policy.

subsections.title: Safe Handling of Sharps
subsections.content: Do NOT recap used needles, do NOT remove needles from syringes by hand, and do NOT bend or break used needles.  Place syringes, lancets, needles and other sharps promptly in puncture‑resistant containers.

sections.title: Reprocessing Single‑Use Devices
sections.content: All single‑use devices should be used once, but in resource‑limited settings they are often reused.  Departmental policy must be strictly followed; cleaning must occur promptly after use.  Respiratory circuits, tubing and equipment are commonly reprocessed, whereas syringes, needles and disposable gloves must never be reprocessed.

sections.title: Key Facts for Providers – Infection Prevention
items: Hospitals, wards, equipment and staff are all sources of infection for a baby, the family and health workers.
Infections cause major morbidity, mortality and prolonged hospital stays; neonates are especially vulnerable.
Control of HAI must include hand hygiene, environmental cleanliness, medical‑equipment maintenance, and waste disposal.
The two most important forms of infection control are hand washing and health screening.
Hand hygiene is the single most important measure to reduce transmitting infections.
Health‑care workers and guardians at risk of transmitting or acquiring infections should not be on the ward.
Facility dress code should be adhered to prevent infections.
Infection‑prevention and cleaning protocols must be followed for equipment, linen, feed preparations and all procedures.
Isolation facilities should be available for high‑risk or infectious patients.

title: Resuscitation of the Newborn (Chapter 3)
learningObjectives: Describe which newborns may need more than essential newborn care at delivery (require resuscitation)
Prepare for providing care at birth
Provide care at birth for all newborns
Describe essential newborn care at delivery
Resuscitate babies who need more than essential newborn care
Utilise all equipment necessary for resuscitation (Radiant warmer, Suction Machine, Pulse oximeter, Glucometer, Oxygen concentrator, Oxygen cylinder)

sections.title: Why is care at birth important?
sections.content: This is the critical period of transition from intra‑utero life to extra‑utero independent existence. Effective care at birth is needed to anticipate problems with this transition and to provide support to ensure stabilization. Most babies born with apnoea at birth will start to breathe themselves within 60–90 seconds if they have a clear airway.

sections.title: Which babies require resuscitation?
sections.content: Approximately 10 % of newborns require some assistance to begin breathing at birth; very few, only about 1 % need more than basic resuscitation to survive.

sections.title: High Risk Deliveries
sections.content: These are deliveries where it is more likely that resuscitation will be required. They include deliveries to mothers who are sick, who had a previous foetal or neonatal death, with pre‑eclampsia or eclampsia, with multiple pregnancies, or who are delivering preterm. Delivery circumstances that increase the risk are abnormal presentation, prolonged second stage, prolapsed cord, prolonged rupture of membranes, and meconium staining of the liquor.

sections.title: Preparation for a delivery
sections.content: For many infants, resuscitation cannot be anticipated before delivery. Therefore be prepared for resuscitation at **every** delivery.

subsections.title: Temperature
subsections.content: The recommended temperature for the delivery room is 25 °C. Shut windows, turn off AC/fans, warm the resuscitaire 30 min before delivery and have at least two pre‑warmed absorbent towels – one to dry the baby and one to wrap the baby.

subsections.title: Personnel
subsections.content: Nurse/midwife should identify a helper, explain roles and allocate duties according to skill level (qualified staff, untrained staff, or relative).

subsections.title: Equipment
subsections.content: Check that all equipment and supplies are available, clean and functional before birth. Equipment should be positioned for immediate use.

images.caption: Check the bag against your hand
…before using it on a baby

sections.title: Baby is Delivered
sections.content: Most newborns require only simple supportive care at and after delivery. Deliver the baby onto the mother’s abdomen, note the time of birth and wait one minute before clamping the pulsating cord. Use two dry warm cloths – one to dry and a fresh one to wrap. Immediate drying prevents heat loss and often stimulates breathing.

subsections.title: How do you determine whether the baby requires resuscitation?
subsections.content: Assessment‑Decision table (crying → routine care; not crying but chest rising → routine care; gasping, not breathing, or very poor tone → start resuscitation immediately).

subsections.title: Key Facts for Providers – How to Provide Essential Newborn Care at Delivery
subsections.content: 1. Dry the infant with a clean cloth and observe. 2. Maintain skin‑to‑skin contact. 3. Cover to prevent heat loss. 4. Clamp and cut cord after ≥1 min. 5. Encourage breast‑feeding within the first hour. 6. Skin‑to‑skin & early breast‑feeding keep infant warm and prevent hypoglycaemia.

images.caption: Two clothes – one to dry and a fresh one to wrap

sections.title: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation
sections.content: If the baby requires resuscitation provide warmth with a radiant warmer or a warming cot. Position yourself at the bedside (head of the baby) so the chest and abdomen remain unobstructed.

subsections.title: ABCs of Resuscitation
subsections.content: A – Ensure Airway is open and clear. B – Ensure Breathing (spontaneous or assisted). C – Ensure adequate Circulation of oxygenated blood. Maintain body temperature throughout.

subsections.title: A. Airway
subsections.content: Open airway by slightly extending the neck, maintain neutral position, use a rolled cloth if occiput is large. Clear secretions by wiping or suctioning (mouth‑first, then nose) with a Penguin suction device or catheter.

images.caption: Correct head position to open up airway for bag ventilation – do not hyperextend the neck.
Suction the mouth and nose (M before N) using Penguin suction device.
To empty during suction
To prepare for cleaning after use

subsections.title: B. Positive‑Pressure Ventilation (Bag‑Mask)
subsections.content: Indications: baby not breathing or gasping, HR <100 bpm despite spontaneous breathing, persistent central cyanosis. Use appropriately sized masks (round or anatomically shaped). Position mask covering nose, mouth and chin; hold with thumb‑index‑middle fingers and lift the chin with ring/fifth fingers. Bag‑mask has a pop‑off valve set at ~30‑40 cm H₂O to prevent excessive pressure. Deliver 40‑60 breaths/min (≈1 breath/sec). For term infants use room air; for preterm ≤32 weeks start with 30 % O₂ (or air if blender unavailable). Assess effectiveness by chest rise, heart‑rate >100 bpm, improved colour and spontaneous effort.

images.caption: Ventilating a neonate with bag and mask – pull the jaw forward with the third finger; do not hyperextend the neck.
The self‑inflating bag with pop‑off valve.

subsections.title: C. Circulation – Chest Compressions
subsections.content: Begin compressions if HR <60 bpm after 30 seconds of effective ventilation. Two providers are needed – one for ventilation (head of the baby) and one for compressions (side or foot). Techniques: Thumb technique (thumbs on sternum, fingers under back) or 2‑finger technique (middle finger + index/ring finger). Position hands on the lower third of the sternum (halfway between xiphoid and line between nipples). Compress to a depth of ~1⁄3 AP chest diameter, release fully. Ratio 3 compressions : 1 ventilation (3:1) ≈ 90 compressions/min. After ~30 seconds reassess HR. If HR >60 bpm stop compressions, continue ventilation at 40‑60 breaths/min. If HR remains <60 bpm continue CPR until HR >100 bpm or the baby is deemed non‑viable.

images.caption: Thumb technique – thumbs on sternum, fingers under back.
2‑finger technique – tips of middle finger + index/ring finger on sternum.
Hand position – lower third of sternum, half way between xiphoid and nipple line.
Place thumbs/fingers just above the xiphoid. Avoid direct pressure on the xiphoid.
Depth of compression ≈ one‑third of anterior‑posterior chest diameter.
Compression‑ventilation ratio 3:1.

sections.title: Cessation of Resuscitation
sections.content: Discontinue efforts when: 1) infant is not breathing and no detectable heartbeat after 10 min; or 2) no spontaneous breathing and HR < 60 bpm after 20 min of effective resuscitation. Record the event, inform the parents and allow them to hold the infant if they wish.

title: Resuscitation of the Young Infant (Chapter 4)
learningObjectives: Demonstrate the resuscitation of a sick young infant.

sections.title: Managing Airway and Breathing
sections.description: The letters A and B in ABCCCD represent airway and breathing. The clinician must assess airway patency, breathing effort, central cyanosis and signs of severe respiratory distress.

subsections.title: A. Airway
details: Neck is slightly extended; head tilt‑chin‑lift maneuver is used.
Conscious infant: inspect mouth, remove secretions, let infant find a comfortable position, give oxygen, continue assessment.
Unconscious infant: open airway with slight head tilt and chin lift, inspect mouth, remove secretions, check chest rise, insert airway if needed.

subsections.title: Insertion of an Oropharyngeal (Guedel) Airway
steps: Select an appropriately sized airway (extends from angle of mouth to angle of jaw with convex side up).
Position the child to open the airway.
Insert the airway with the convex side up.
Re‑check airway opening.
If the airway is not patent, try a different size or reposition.

subsections.title: B. Breathing
signsOfSevereRespiratoryDistress: Unable to feed due to distress
subsections.oxygenManagement: Give oxygen via catheter/prongs; if distress worsens or central cyanosis persists, add high‑flow oxygen (≈4 L/min) via face mask.

bagMaskVentilation.description: If spontaneous ventilation is inadequate, ventilate with a self‑inflating bag and mask.
tips: Use a neutral head position; avoid hyper‑extension.
Place padding under the shoulders to prevent occipital flexion.
After five effective breaths, check femoral pulse for 10 seconds; if absent, begin chest compressions.

subsections.title: C. Chest Compressions
subsections.description: Chest compressions are performed as in neonatal resuscitation: 15 compressions followed by 2 ventilation breaths per cycle.
subsections.note: Respiratory arrest (breathless but with a pulse) is often salvageable; a true cardiorespiratory arrest (no pulse) is rarely survivable.

title: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
learningObjectives: Describe how to manage a neonate with respiratory distress.
Define apnoea and describe how to treat and prevent apnoea.
Describe how a neonate and young infant with difficulties in breathing may present.
List the most likely causes of breathing difficulties in neonates and young infants.
Describe the management of the young infant with breathing difficulties.
Know and use equipment required for management of babies with difficulty breathing.

sections.title: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn
sections.content: Why are breathing difficulties in the newborn so important? Breathing difficulties are the most common way that sick neonates present to the healthcare worker. There are several different possible diagnoses and these need to be considered in order to provide the correct management.

subsections.title: Signs and Symptoms
items: Fast breathing – respiratory rate of more than 60 bpm

subsections.title: Possible Causes – Newborn
items: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) – common in premature neonates
Transient Tachypnoea of the Newborn (TTN) – common after Caesarean or quick delivery
Sepsis – more common in very premature or with risk factors
Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn (PPHN)

subsections.title: Respiratory Distress Syndrome
subsections.content: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) occurs primarily in premature infants; its incidence is inversely related to gestational age and birthweight. It occurs in 60–80 % of infants < 28 weeks, 15–30 % of those 32‑36 weeks, about 5 % beyond 37 weeks and rarely at term. Surfactant deficiency is the primary cause. Risk is increased by maternal diabetes, multiple births, Caesarean section, precipitous delivery, asphyxia, cold stress and a history of previously affected infants; reduced by antenatal steroid use. Management includes oxygen, CPAP, ventilation, antibiotics and NGT. Newborns usually develop RDS within 3 days of birth.

subsections.title: Management of Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS)
items: Give oxygen via nasal cannula 0.5‑1 L/min (escalate as needed, consider early CPAP).
Give antibiotics if distress persists after 4 h or if sepsis, pneumonia or meconium aspiration is suspected.
Position thorax 30° upright to ease breathing.
Treat the underlying cause if identified.
subsections.notes: Consider early CPAP if severe distress does not improve; CPAP is recommended for preterm newborns with RDS as soon as the diagnosis is made.

subsections.title: Apnoea
subsections.content: Definition: cessation of breathing for longer than 20 seconds, possibly with bradycardia. May be due to prematurity or secondary to other conditions such as respiratory distress, infection, cold‑stress, low blood volume, hypoglycaemia, CNS disorders, perinatal compromise or prematurity.

subsections.title: Apnoea – Investigations (after clinical exam)
items: Sepsis work‑up (blood culture, urine dipstick & culture, lumbar puncture, chest X‑ray, full blood count, urea, creatinine and electrolytes)

subsections.title: Apnoea – Treatment
subsections.content: Determine cause and treat. General measures: tactile stimulation, correct anaemia, maintain normal temperature, correct electrolyte imbalance, look for intraventricular haemorrhage, sepsis, PDA, necrotising enterocolitis, GER and treat accordingly. Give aminophylline for prevention of apnoea of prematurity (6 mg/kg PO loading dose then 2.5 mg/kg BD PO). Caffeine is preferred where available.

subsections.title: Equipment for Management of a Baby with Breathing Problems

sections.title: Breathing Difficulties in the Young Infant
sections.content: Young infants with breathing difficulties present with fast breathing and feeding difficulties. On examination there may be grunting, head nodding, cyanosis, intercostal/sternal/subcostal recession; auscultation may reveal crepitations and/or wheezes.

subsections.title: Main Differential Diagnoses
items: Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PJP) – if HIV exposed

subsections.title: Supportive Treatment – Nutrition
subsections.content: If breathing < 60 bpm, attempt breastfeeding. If the baby is struggling, switch to oro‑ or nasogastric tube feeds. If breathing 60‑80 bpm, give oro‑/nasogastric feeds every two hours with expressed breast milk (restricted maintenance volumes). If breathing > 80 bpm, consider IV fluids 100 mL/kg/day.

subsections.title: Oxygen Therapy – Escalate Stepwise
subsections.content: Ensure airway is clear. Start oxygen via nasal prongs/catheter 0.5 L/min. Re‑assess after 15‑30 min; if SpO₂ < 90 % increase to 1 L/min (max 2 L/min). Re‑assess after another 30 min; if still < 90 % give 4 L/min via face mask. Consider CPAP if no improvement and no contraindications.

subsections.title: Specific Treatment
subsections.content: Treat with IV antibiotics if pneumonia is suspected (see wall charts for doses). Give high‑dose cotrimoxazole and steroids if PJP is suspected (see formulary for doses).

subsections.title: Pulse Oximeter
subsections.content: Should be used for all sick or at‑risk patients and for anyone receiving oxygen therapy, CPAP, or assisted ventilation. See the dedicated Pulse Oximeter page for more information.

subsections.title: Oxygen Concentrator
subsections.content: Used when oxygen is needed for one or two patients or to share oxygen between multiple patients via a flow splitter. Indicated for sick children with hypoxia (SpO₂ < 90 %). See the Oxygen Concentrator page for more information.

subsections.title: Oxygen Cylinder
subsections.content: Provides supplemental oxygen directly to hypoxic patients, can be shared via a flow splitter, or used with devices such as CPAP. See the Oxygen Cylinder page for more information.

subsections.title: Oxygen Splitter
subsections.content: Allows oxygen from a single source to be delivered to more than one hypoxic patient at low flows. See the Oxygen Splitter page for more information.
