keyPoints: Poor feeding due to respiratory distress
Cyanosis (confirm with pulse oximeter if available)

sections.title: Delivery Methods of Oxygen
sections.content: Describes the sources of oxygen (concentrator or cylinder) and preferred delivery devices (nasal prongs → nasal catheter → face mask). Explains step‑wise escalation and monitoring.

subsections.title: Stepwise escalation of oxygen therapy according to the YI condition
subsections.content: Provides a flow‑chart style escalation from low‑flow nasal prongs up to bCPAP and, if needed, investigation for pneumothorax.

images.description: Flow‑chart illustrating stepwise escalation of oxygen therapy.

subsections.title: Monitoring and ongoing care of the patient
subsections.content: Checklist for mothers and staff to ensure safe delivery of oxygen and early detection of problems.
checklist: Displacement of the prongs or catheter

sections.title: When and How to Stop Oxygen Therapy
sections.content: Guidelines for weaning oxygen based on stable saturations and clinical status, with a step‑wise reduction protocol and safety checks.
weaningSteps: Reduce flow by 0.5 L/min; re‑check SpO₂ & clinical condition after 15 min.
If stable, repeat reduction by 0.5 L/min with 15‑min checks.
If SpO₂ < 90 % or clinical deterioration, return to previous flow level.
discontinuationCriteria: SpO₂ > 95 % on 0.5 L/min and clinically stable.
Gentle removal of tape and nasal prongs.
Re‑check SpO₂ after 15 min; restart if saturation drops or clinical status worsens.
specialConsiderations: If pulse oximetry unavailable, use clinical signs (no increased work of breathing, normal RR, no tiring).
Avoid SpO₂ > 96 % in premature infants – risk of retinopathy of prematurity, brain & lung injury. Target 90‑94 % when on supplemental O₂ or CPAP.

sections.title: Sources and delivery methods of oxygen
sections.content: Overview of the equipment used to provide oxygen to NYI, with brief how‑to notes for each device.

subsections.title: Oxygen concentrator
subsections.content: Main source; delivers 5‑10 L/min, adjustable concentration gauge; filter cleaning weekly; keep SpO₂ > 90 %.

images.description: Diagram of a typical oxygen concentrator.

subsections.title: Nasal prongs
subsections.content: Preferred device; various sizes, placed just inside nostrils and taped; keep nostrils clear; 0.5‑1 L/min gives ~30‑35 % FiO₂; aim SpO₂ > 92 %.

subsections.title: Nasal tube or catheter
subsections.content: Use 8 Fr catheter; measure insertion depth from nostril to inner eyebrow margin; 0.5‑1 L/min gives ~30‑35 % FiO₂; aim SpO₂ > 90 %.

images.description: Diagram showing correct catheter length measurement.

subsections.title: Face mask
subsections.content: Used for severe distress; delivers 40‑60 % FiO₂ at ≥4 L/min; reservoir mask can deliver 100 % (resuscitation); may require one concentrator per infant; aim SpO₂ > 90 %.

sections.title: E 6 Oxygen Therapy (Supplementary)
sections.content: Brief note that oxygen sources can be shared via a flow splitter and combined with CPAP. Supplemental O₂ is indicated for sick children with SpO₂ < 90 %. References to additional modules.

title: Introduction to Bubble Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (bCPAP) (Chapter 7 / E7)
learningObjectives: Define bCPAP.
List possible indications and contraindications for bCPAP.
Describe how bCPAP works.
Initiate a baby on bCPAP.
Describe how to escalate and wean a baby on bCPAP.
Explain infection‑prevention measures for bCPAP.
List possible complications of bCPAP.
Briefly explain the basic maintenance of a bCPAP machine.
Identify troubleshooting steps and basic repairs for bCPAP.

sections.title: Bubble Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (bCPAP)
sections.content: A process of giving continuous flow of air under regulated pressure through the airway.  It is a constant pressure applied to the airway, generated by continuous, consistent flow of air with the aim of opening collapsed lung segments and maintaining patency in already opened air spaces.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.content: Bubble CPAP (bCPAP) devices provide both positive pressure & increased fraction of oxygen (FiO₂) to newborns with respiratory distress.  They are especially useful for premature babies with RDS and other conditions that increase work of breathing.

subsections.title: Indications & Contra‑indications
subsections.content: Indications include RDS, neonatal pneumonia, severe TTN, PPHN, apnoea of prematurity, meconium aspiration, neonatal sepsis, etc.  Contra‑indications include significant mouth/nose bleeding, anatomical abnormalities (choanal atresia, cleft lip/palate), obvious malformations incompatible with life, presence of pneumothorax, and severe hypoxic‑ischemic encephalopathy.

subsections.title: Alert 1.1 – When to initiate bCPAP vs low‑flow oxygen
subsections.content: Early bCPAP is recommended for pre‑term infants <1.5 kg with any respiratory distress on the day of birth.  Evidence shows it reduces need for mechanical ventilation, improves surfactant production and decreases mortality across income settings.

subsections.title: Alert 1.2 – bCPAP & low‑flow oxygen context
subsections.content: Supplemental oxygen at supra‑optimal doses is associated with ROP, BPD, periventricular leukomalacia and prolonged ventilation.  SpO₂ target of 90‑95 % is recommended.

subsections.title: TRY bCPAP Algorithm
subsections.content: A flow‑chart used by nurses/clinicians to decide who would benefit from bCPAP and how to prioritise when resources are limited.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content: Bubble CPAP devices use a pump to deliver a blend of air and oxygen at a continuous positive pressure that keeps airway spaces open, increases alveolar recruitment and reduces work of breathing.

subsections.title: Device Components
subsections.content: Pump, inspiratory tube, expiratory tube, pressure regulator (water bottle).

subsections.title: Alert 2.1 – Use of humidification in bCPAP
subsections.content: Heated humidification may improve comfort and reduce mucosal injury but may increase infection risk, cost and resource burden.  Evidence is mixed; benefits appear greatest for infants <1 kg.

subsections.title: Alert 2.2 – SpO₂ & Safe Oxygen Delivery
subsections.content: Target SpO₂ of 90‑95 % minimizes complications of both hypoxia and hyperoxia.

sections.title: Management
sections.content: Covers set‑up, patient preparation, initiation, routine care, escalation and weaning of bCPAP.

subsections.title: Setting up for a patient
subsections.content: Collect machine, power cable, tubing, prongs, connectors, oxygen source; position device; fill water bottle to desired pressure (typically 6 cm H₂O); connect inspiratory/expiratory tubing; attach prongs; turn on device; open oxygen flowmeter; test bubbling.

subsections.title: Preparing a patient
subsections.content: Warm baby, explain procedure, hand‑wash, gather hat/stockinette, OGT, gloves, syringe, tape, suction catheter, correctly sized prongs.  Perform suction if needed, insert OGT, verify placement with aspirate/litmus or whoosh test, select appropriate prong size.

subsections.title: Starting a patient
subsections.content: Turn on device, attach oxygen, place hat, set initial pressure (≈6 cm H₂O), total flow 6 L/min, FiO₂ ≈ 50 %.  Adjust oxygen/total flow using the blending table.  Test bubbling, place saline drops in nostrils, insert prongs to the marked line, secure tubing with hat clips or rubber bands & safety pins, check for bubbling, monitor vitals after 15 min then every 4 h.

subsections.title: Caring for a patient
subsections.content: Every 4 h (or more frequently) check vitals, work of breathing, nasal blockages, abdominal distension, nasal septum trauma.  Provide saline drops, inspect prongs/tubing/hat, adjust water level in bottle, replace water daily, and use the DOPE mnemonic (Displacement, Obstruction, Patient problem, Equipment failure) before any escalation.

subsections.title: Increasing bCPAP
subsections.content: Follow the “Increasing bCPAP Treatment” algorithm; ensure device is functioning, assess patient, then increment pressure or flow as indicated.

subsections.title: Removing a patient (Weaning)
subsections.content: Follow the “Weaning a Patient from bCPAP Treatment” flow‑chart; reduce pressure/flow gradually based on stability, then discontinue when criteria are met.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
sections.content: Hand hygiene, use of new or properly re‑processed tubing, storage of consumables, and thorough disinfection after each use are essential to avoid equipment‑related infections.

subsections.title: General Infection Prevention
subsections.content: Clean hands before/after patient contact; ensure all patient‑related tubing is new or cleaned and dried; store consumables in a clean dry area; avoid kinks in tubing.

subsections.title: Alert 4.1 – Re‑processing single‑use devices
subsections.content: Single‑use circuits should be re‑processed promptly with a documented standard of practice; delays increase infection risk.

subsections.title: Disinfection After Use
subsections.content: Dispose of water in the bottle, discard hat, clean tubing if reused, wipe the exterior of the device with alcohol or diluted chlorine, and disinfect flowmeter controls with 70 % alcohol.

sections.title: Complications
sections.content: Both clinical and device‑related complications can arise from bCPAP use.

subsections.title: Clinical Complications
subsections.content: Nasal blockage, necrotic nasal septum, gastric distension, pneumothorax, decreased cardiac output.

subsections.title: Device Complications
subsections.content: Pressure leakages (no bubbling), power failure, tubing kinks, loose connections.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
sections.content: Basic preventive care ensures longevity of the bCPAP unit.

subsections.title: Power Source
subsections.content: Use mains power; ideally with backup power to prevent interruptions.

subsections.title: Ward Location
subsections.content: Secure the device near an oxygen source, visible, stable, and away from vibration‑inducing equipment.

subsections.title: User Preventive Maintenance
subsections.content: Run the device weekly with total flow = 10 L/min, oxygen = 2 L/min for at least 15 min to verify function and minimise internal contamination.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
sections.content: First‑line steps to resolve common errors before contacting engineering.

subsections.title: The device does not turn on
steps: Check power cable is securely attached and plugged in.
Confirm the socket is switched on.
If still no power, contact maintenance.

subsections.title: Silver balls in the flowmeter do not rise
steps: Tap the front of the flowmeter firmly with a knuckle or screwdriver handle.
If the balls still do not move, request cleaning of the flowmeter and verification of internal tubing.

subsections.title: Total flowmeter does not go up to 10 L/min
steps: Contact maintenance for an internal filter change.

subsections.title: Water in the bottle is not bubbling
steps: Confirm prongs fully fill the nostrils and the patient’s mouth is closed.
If prongs are correct, occlude them with a finger; if still no bubbling, inspect the patient‑port seal (replace if cracked).

sections.title: Appendix 1 – Heated & Humidified Air in Non‑Invasive Ventilation
sections.content: Discusses physiological rationale, potential benefits (comfort, reduced airway injury) and drawbacks (infection risk, cost) of humidification in bCPAP.  Current evidence is mixed; humidification appears most important for infants <1 kg but is not universally required.

sections.title: References
sections.content: A comprehensive list of 81 literature citations supporting the chapter content.

learningObjectives.text: Describe the management of babies who have Hypoxic Ischaemic Encephalopathy

sections.title: Hypoxic Ischemic Encephalopathy (HIE)
content: HIE is also called birth asphyxia and neonatal encephalopathy. It is very common in Malawi, making optimal management of these newborns essential.

Lack of oxygen supply to organs before, during, or immediately after birth results in HIE, recognised by delayed onset of breathing or crying. Clinical features that evolve over the first 2‑3 days of life include irritability, lethargy or coma, hypotonia or hypertonia, convulsions, apnoea, temperature instability, poor suck, and feeding difficulty. Diagnosis in pre‑term infants is difficult because prematurity itself can cause hypotonia.
Suspect HIE in a baby with some or all of the following:
- Foetal bradycardia
- Prolonged second stage of labour
- Required bag‑and‑mask ventilation for > 5 min
- Required cardiac massage
- Irritability, hypotonia, seizures, poor suck, poor colour
- High lactate on cord blood
- APGAR score < 7 at 5 min

sections.title: Scoring System for APGAR Score
content: The APGAR score is used to assess the newborn’s condition at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. A score < 7 at 5 minutes is a red flag for possible HIE.

sections.title: Classification of Severity of Hypoxic Ischaemic Encephalopathy (HIE)
content: Severity is classified into mild, moderate, and severe based on clinical features (consciousness, tone, seizures, and sucking/respiration). Babies with mild or moderate HIE generally have a good prognosis, whereas severe HIE is associated with poor outcomes.

tables.title: Features by severity
rows: Unable to sustain adequate regular spontaneous respiration

subsections.title: Distinguishing Between Convulsions and Jitteriness
content: Most children with HIE present with convulsions; however, these may be subtle and need to be differentiated from jitteriness.

tables.title: Convulsions vs. Jitteriness
rows: Both fast and slow components; slow movements (1‑3 jerks per second).
Fast movements (4‑6 per second); tremors are of equal amplitude.
Often associated with eye movements (tonic deviation of the pupil) or blinking.
Not associated with eye movements or autonomic changes.

sections.title: Management of Birth Asphyxia / HIE
content: Management focuses on supportive care, seizure control, maintenance of temperature, glucose, fluids, and feeding, and careful documentation and follow‑up.

subsections.title: Key Facts for Providers – Supportive Management of Birth Asphyxia
content: Check for emergency signs (ABC) and provide emergency care.
Place the baby under a radiant warmer to maintain normal temperature while preventing hyperthermia.
Check blood glucose; treat hypoglycaemia if present, otherwise re‑check every 12 hours.
Provide maintenance intravenous fluids using wall charts after stabilising emergency signs.
Consider enteral feeding if no emergency signs or abdominal distension. Initiate breast feeding if sucking is good; otherwise, tube‑feed expressed breast milk using wall‑chart‑derived volumes.

subsections.title: Management of Convulsions
content: Refer to the dedicated convulsion management protocol (link provided in the original document).

imageLinks.description: Flow diagram of HIE management (zoom for details)

sections.title: Documentation, Discharge and Follow‑up
content: Document the case in the health passport and classify HIE as mild, moderate, or severe.
If severe, arrange a physiotherapy appointment (initially floppy infants often become stiff, requiring the mother to learn massage and basic physiotherapy skills).
Schedule follow‑up at 6 months focusing on head growth, general health, and motor neuro‑development. Further assessments at 12‑24 months should concentrate on cognitive and language development.

globalImageLinks.description: Management flow diagram for Birth Asphyxia/HIE

title: Care of the Low Birth Weight/Preterm Infant (Chapter 9)
learningObjectives: List the main problems associated with low birth weight (LBW) / preterm baby.
Describe the management of the feeds for a LBW/preterm baby.
Describe the steps involved in tube feeding.
Counsel the mother on discharge, home care and follow‑up.

sections.title: Why is care of the low birth weight infant so important in Malawi?
sections.content: In 2018 the WHO estimated that 10.5 % of babies born in Malawi are low‑birth‑weight, the majority being premature. A neonate < 2500 g is classified as LBW. Nearly 75 % of neonatal deaths occur among LBW neonates. Even after surviving the acute phase they remain at higher risk of malnutrition, recurrent infections and neuro‑developmental impairment. LBW may be caused by prematurity, intra‑uterine growth retardation (IUGR) or both; distinguishing the aetiology guides further management.

sections.title: Common problems in LBW, preterm and SGA neonates
sections.content: Increased risk of:

subsections.title: How to estimate the gestational age
subsections.content: When the exact gestational age is unknown the Parkin score (physical‑characteristics scoring) is used. The total score is matched to a gestational‑age table.

tables.title: Approximate Gestational Age Based on Physical Characteristics (Parkin Score)
rows: Breast tissue palpable on one/both sides < 0.5 cm
Smooth, medium thickness, rash & superficial peeling
Breast tissue palpable on both sides 0.5‑1 cm
Slight thickening, superficial cracking, especially hands/feet

tables.title: Gestational Age by Total Parkin Score

sections.title: Management at Delivery of Low Birth Weight / Premature Babies

subsections.title: Deciding where a LBW baby should be managed
subsections.content: A healthy LBW newborn ≥ 1500 g can be cared for at home under supervision of a health‑care worker. Babies < 1500 g are at risk of hypothermia, feeding problems, apnoea, RDS and necrotising enterocolitis and usually need facility‑based care with close monitoring.

subsections.title: Indications for hospitalisation of a LBW neonate
listItems: A sick neonate with danger signs
A LBW neonate who is not gaining weight
Neonate unable to feed from the breast or by cup

subsections.title: Keeping the LBW babies dry and warm (prevention of hypothermia)
subsections.content: Continuous Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) = skin‑to‑skin contact with the mother, preferably with exclusive breast‑milk feeding. Intermittent KMC = skin‑to‑skin alternated with warming devices. Three categories of KMC in Malawi:
listItems: Facility KMC – for all neonates < 1500 g
Ambulatory KMC – babies 1500‑1800 g after discharge, followed up by the facility
Community KMC – babies > 1800 g, clinically stable, cared for in the community
subsections.keyFacts: If continuous or intermittent KMC is not possible, use an overhead radiant warmer, incubator or warming crib. Keep the room at ~25 °C, use a hat, change wet clothing frequently and monitor axillary temperature.

subsections.title: Key facts for providers & mothers/guardians – skin‑to‑skin contact (KMC)
subsections.content: Place baby upright in mother's clothing, ensure baby’s head is turned to one side, tie securely, cover both with mother's clothes. Allow baby to breast‑feed at least every 2 h, keep mother propped up, add blankets if room is cold, enlist another caregiver for KMC when mother rests.

subsections.title: Breast‑milk as preferred feed
subsections.content: Breast‑milk provides high electrolytes, protein, antibodies and anti‑infective factors essential for rapid growth and survival of preterm infants.

tables.title: Ideal feeding regime by birth‑weight / gestation

subsections.title: Key facts for providers & mothers/guardians – feeding LBW / premature infants
subsections.content: Feeding should be scheduled because preterm infants rarely demand feeds. Work out a schedule with the mother; LBW babies may take longer on the breast.

subsections.title: Likely route of feeding according to birth‑weight / gestational age
Breast‑milk expression – key facts for providers & mothers/guardians
subsections.content: Mothers should express milk when the baby cannot breast‑feed directly, to maintain milk supply and relieve engorgement. Detailed step‑by‑step technique is listed in the source document.

subsections.title: Nasogastric (NG) and Oro‑gastric (OG) tube feeding
subsections.content: Use French size 5‑6 tubes. Measure from nose (or mouth) to ear lobe then to midpoint between xiphisternum and umbilicus. Verify placement by aspirating gastric contents and auscultation.

subsections.title: Steps of oro‑/nasogastric tube feeding
orderedList: Check tube position before each feed.
Use a clean syringe, remove the plunger.
Pinch the tube, connect syringe barrel to tube end.
Fill syringe barrel with required milk volume.
Hold tube with one hand, release pinch, elevate syringe 5‑10 cm above the baby.
Allow milk to flow by gravity; do not use the plunger.
Control flow by adjusting syringe height.
Observe the baby throughout the feed; never leave unattended.
Do not flush tube with water or saline after feed.
Cap the tube between feeds.
Progress to cup/spoon when baby can swallow without coughing.
Replace tube after 3 days or sooner if pulled out/blocked.

subsections.title: Steps of cup feeding (id="cup")
orderedList: Ensure baby is awake, semi‑upright; place cloth to catch drips.
Measure the required milk volume into the cup.
Rest the cup tip on the baby’s lower lip.
Tilt the cup to dispense a small amount at a time.
Feed slowly, confirming each swallow before the next sip.
Stop when the baby closes the mouth; never force feed.
Document time and volume fed.

subsections.title: Prevention of apnoea
subsections.content: Refer to the linked section on apnoea in the main textbook; no additional detail is given in this chapter.

sections.title: Vitamin supplements and iron for preterm infants

tables.title: Supplement regimen
rows: 0.5 mg IM in preterm neonates; 1 mg IM in term neonates
0.3‑0.6 ml (5‑10 drops) per day (≈ 1000 IU vitamin A & 400 IU vitamin D)
When taking full feeds until 6 months
2‑4 mg/kg per day oral (usually 0.5 ml of 10 mg ml⁻¹ syrup for a 2 kg baby)
Start at 2 weeks of age, continue until 6 months

sections.title: Discharge of the LBW/Preterm Infant
keyFacts: A well LBW baby can be discharged when:
Fully breast‑fed (or breast‑feeding supplemented by expressed milk via cup) and has gained weight for 3 consecutive days.
Has had no apnoea for seven days.
Mother feels confident caring for the baby.
BCG and oral polio doses recorded.
Weekly visits until infant reaches 2.5 kg, then as per routine schedule.
Same schedule as term infants if baby is well; delayed until recovery if baby is sick.
Exclusive breast‑feeding, keeping baby warm, recognising danger signs, maternal nutrition & health.

title: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe
learningObjectives: Explain why a syringe driver is required for neonatal intravenous fluid therapy.
Identify the clinical problems that indicate the need for a syringe driver.
Perform a complete pre‑use assessment of a neonate before starting a syringe driver.
Demonstrate step‑by‑step set‑up, preparation, start‑up, ongoing care and removal of a syringe driver.
Apply infection‑prevention practices for all components of the syringe driver system.
Recognise common clinical and device complications associated with syringe‑driver use.
Carry out routine care, preventive maintenance and basic troubleshooting of a syringe driver.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Recognise that all neonates requiring IV fluids for maintenance or resuscitation need a syringe driver.
sections.content: All babies who require administration of intravenous fluids, especially for maintenance or resuscitation will need a syringe driver to deliver the fluids.

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Identify neonates at risk of hypoglycaemia and dehydration when they are not being fed.
Understand how syringe drivers control low‑volume fluid delivery over minutes or hours.
sections.content:
Patients who are not being fed are at risk of developing hypoglycaemia and dehydration. Syringe drivers are used for the controlled infusion of small volumes of intravenous fluids over minutes or hours.

A syringe driver uses an internal motor to push a syringe plunger at very slow rates to ensure controlled delivery of low volumes of fluid. Syringe drivers may be calibrated for specific syringe brands and sizes and may have internal medicine libraries that can store flow rates and delivery volumes for specific types of fluids.

Patients should receive intravenous fluid per hour, based on their weight and age. Due to their small weights, neonates should never be given fluid too quickly or in too large volumes.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Set up a syringe driver and all required accessories for a patient.
Prepare a cannulated or non‑cannulated neonate for infusion.
Start the infusion safely, verify patency and monitor for early complications.
Provide ongoing care, including 4‑hourly assessments and alarm checks.
Remove a patient safely once therapy is complete.

subsections.title: Setting up for a patient
steps: Collect syringe driver, power cable, clearly labelled syringe of treatment fluid and extension tubing.
Connect the power cable to the syringe driver, plug into socket and turn on the socket.
Place the syringe in the syringe channel, secure with the clamp and move the syringe pusher to the top of the plunger.
Turn on the syringe driver, set the desired flow rate and volume, then press start.
Check that fluid is coming out of the syringe tip, connect extension tubing, flush through the tubing and ensure no air bubbles are present.

subsections.title: Preparing a patient – cannulated
steps: Collect gloves, 70 % alcohol, cotton swab, labelled syringe of normal saline.
Wash hands and put on gloves.
Clean end of cannula with alcohol‑soaked swab.
Flush cannula with 1 mL normal saline; if resistance or puffiness occurs, reassess or replace the line.

subsections.title: Preparing a patient – not cannulated
steps: Collect gloves, gauze or cotton swabs, 70 % alcohol, 24‑gauge IV cannula, tape, syringe of normal saline.
Wash hands and put on gloves.
Clean the arm with alcohol‑soaked gauze, apply a gentle tourniquet (no elastic band).
Insert IV cannula into a vein and wait for a flash of blood.
Continue inserting while gently withdrawing the needle, ensuring blood flow persists.
Flush cannula with 1 mL normal saline; if resistance or puffiness occurs, reassess or replace.
If flush is successful, cap the cannula.
Secure the cannula with tape (do not encircle the limb).
Apply a splint if needed to prevent occlusion of the extension tubing.

subsections.title: Starting a patient
steps: Check that the extension tubing is completely filled with fluid – NO AIR should be present.
Set the syringe driver to the appropriate rate and volume; wait for a drop to form at the tubing tip.
Attach the tubing to the cannula.
After 30 minutes, assess the cannula site for redness, swelling or displacement. If the site is puffy or the driver alarms:
Stop the syringe driver.
Flush the cannula to assess patency; if not patent, remove and replace in a different extremity.

subsections.title: Caring for a patient
items: Assess at least every 4 hours for: puffiness/redness around the cannula, cannula displacement, vital signs (respiratory rate, heart rate, SpO₂, temperature), fluid balance (input/output chart), blood sugar.
Record the time of each treatment.
Monitor the syringe driver frequently for remaining fluid volume and any alarms (occlusion, etc.).
If syringe fluid finishes and more fluid is required, repeat the “Starting a patient” procedure.

subsections.title: Removing a patient
subsections.content: When fluid delivery is complete and no further infusion is needed, press Stop on the syringe driver, remove the extension tubing and cap the cannula.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Apply routine cleaning and hand‑hygiene practices before and after handling the syringe driver or cannula.
Ensure all consumables (syringes, tubing, cannulas) are new, single‑use and stored cleanly.
Disinfect the syringe driver and its surfaces after each use according to ward guidelines.

subsections.title: General infection prevention
steps: Clean hands with soap and water or alcohol before and after any patient contact; wear gloves throughout the procedure and dispose of them immediately afterwards.
Thoroughly clean the patient’s skin before inserting or adjusting a cannula.
Use only new, patient‑specific consumables; never reuse items that have contacted bio‑fluids.
Store all consumables in a clean, dry location.

subsections.title: Disinfection after use
steps: Turn off and unplug the syringe driver.
Dispose of tubing and syringe in a hazardous‑waste container; clean LCD controls and syringe clamp with an alcohol‑soaked cotton swab after each use.
Clean the housing of the syringe driver according to ward surface‑disinfection guidelines.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Identify clinical complications such as infection, extravasation injury, fluid overload, dehydration/hypoglycaemia and air emboli.
Recognise device‑related complications, especially errors related to syringe type and calibration.

subsections.title: Clinical complications
items: Infection – inadequate skin cleaning, long‑term indwelling cannula, contaminated tubing or syringe.
Extravasation injury – cannula displacement or occlusion while infusion continues.
Fluid overload – infusion too rapid or too large a volume, leading to oedema or respiratory distress.
Dehydration and hypoglycaemia – interruptions in fluid delivery due to line occlusion or device failure.
Air emboli – presence of air in syringe or tubing can cause severe respiratory or cardiac events.

subsections.title: Device complications
subsections.content: Errors related to syringe type may cause inaccurate fluid volumes because the driver may be calibrated for a specific brand or size of syringe.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Understand the power source requirements and the importance of battery readiness.
Know appropriate storage locations and securing methods for syringe drivers in the ward.
Perform weekly preventive maintenance tests to ensure the device remains functional.

subsections.title: Power source
subsections.content: Syringe drivers are usually mains‑powered with a rechargeable battery. Keep the device on its charger when not in use, but remove it only when needed to ensure a charged battery is available during power outages.

subsections.title: Ward location
subsections.content: Drivers may be wall‑ or pole‑mounted for easy access. Multiple drivers can be used on a single patient; they must be secured while in use and syringes clearly labelled by content.

subsections.title: User preventive maintenance
subsections.content: Set up the driver as if preparing for a patient and run it for 15 minutes each week if not in clinical use. Visually inspect for missing parts or alarm indicators during this test run.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Follow first‑line troubleshooting steps before contacting engineering or maintenance.
Identify common faults such as power failure or inability to push fluid and know appropriate actions.

subsections.title: 1 – The syringe driver is not turning on
steps: Check that the power cable is securely attached to the driver and that the socket is powered.
If the driver still does not turn on, contact the maintenance department.

subsections.title: 2 – The syringe driver is not pushing out fluid
steps: Check that no alarms are sounding on the driver.
Remove the syringe and attempt to manually push fluid from the syringe.
If fluid does not flow manually, replace the syringe.
If fluid flows manually but the driver still fails, contact the maintenance department.

title: E 11 Phototherapy Lights
learningObjectives: Explain the pathophysiology of neonatal jaundice and why phototherapy is required.
Identify clinical situations that indicate the need for phototherapy.
Describe the physical principles of phototherapy (wavelength, irradiance, device types).
Perform a systematic assessment for phototherapy initiation, including use of the Kramer scale and bilirubin measurements.
Demonstrate step‑by‑step management: set‑up, patient preparation, initiation, ongoing care, and safe discontinuation.
Apply infection‑prevention practices for phototherapy equipment and the newborn environment.
Recognise common clinical and device‑related complications of phototherapy and know how to respond.
Carry out routine maintenance, preventive checks and basic troubleshooting of phototherapy units.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.content: Infants have a large volume of bilirubin due to high red‑cell mass and rapid breakdown. The immature liver often cannot clear bilirubin quickly, leading to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice). Phototherapy is indicated for visible jaundice at birth, jaundice extending below the umbilicus, bilirubin levels above treatment thresholds, and in the presence of prematurity, sepsis, bruising/cephalohaematoma, maternal‑infant blood incompatibility, or G6PD deficiency.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content: Phototherapy uses blue light (425‑475 nm) to convert unconjugated bilirubin into a water‑soluble form. Devices can be overhead, over‑/under‑body, or flexible blankets. LEDs are preferred over halogen/fluorescent bulbs because of efficiency, durability and lower heat output.

subsections.title: Need for Phototherapy
subsections.content: Phototherapy need can be determined by serum bilirubin, transcutaneous bilirubin, or visual assessment using Kramer’s scale. In the absence of serum values, treat any visible jaundice on day 1, or Kramer level 3 on day 2+ (jaundice visible below the umbilicus).

subsections.title: TABLE 2.1 JAUNDICE & PHOTOTHERAPY VS TRANSFUSION
subsections.content: Reference table showing bilirubin thresholds for phototherapy and exchange transfusion for term and pre‑term infants across days of life.

subsections.title: ALERT 2.1 – Physical exam estimation of serum bilirubin
subsections.content: Kramer’s scale is the only studied proxy when serum bilirubin is unavailable. It is most reliable for ruling out levels >12 mg/dL in term infants using zones 1‑2.

sections.title: Management
sections.content: Guidelines for using an overhead phototherapy unit: set‑up, patient preparation, start‑up, ongoing care, and removal.

subsections.title: SETTING UP FOR A PATIENT
subsections.content: Collect device, power cable, and light meter. Verify blue light emission. Position meter on mattress. Adjust height (≈20‑30 cm) to achieve therapeutic irradiance (25‑35 µW/cm²).

subsections.title: PREPARING A PATIENT
subsections.content: Explain procedure, perform hand hygiene, gather eye mask/gauze, remove clothing leaving only a minimal diaper, place eye protection securely.

subsections.title: STARTING A PATIENT
subsections.content: Place infant directly under the switched‑on light, document start date/time.

subsections.title: CARING FOR A PATIENT
subsections.content: Provide continuous therapy, monitor vitals, rotate skin every 4 h, check bilirubin (serum or Kramer), ensure hydration, weigh daily, verify eye‑mask integrity, assess for worsening jaundice and adjust irradiance if needed.

alerts.title: ALERT 3.1 – Eye‑mask removal for feeding
alerts.content: When feeding off the light, remove eye mask, check for infection, and encourage mother‑baby bonding.

subsections.title: REMOVING A PATIENT
subsections.content: Discontinue phototherapy when Kramer zones are resolved or bilirubin <50 mmol/L (or 3 mg/dL below treatment threshold). Turn off light, remove eye protection, continue monitoring for 24‑48 h.

alerts.title: ALERT 3.2 – Discontinuation when bilirubin unavailable
alerts.content: Minimum 12 h phototherapy is insufficient; recommend ≥24 h for term infants and longer for pre‑terms. Use cleared conjunctiva or Kramer scale as surrogate.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
sections.content: Routine cleaning and hand hygiene are essential to avoid healthcare‑associated infections with phototherapy equipment.

subsections.title: GENERAL INFECTION PREVENTION
subsections.content: Hand hygiene before/after use, use new or cleaned eye coverings, store equipment dry, avoid cable kinks, single‑patient use per unit.

subsections.title: DISINFECTION AFTER USE
subsections.content: Turn off and unplug unit, disinfect handles and LCD with alcohol, clean housing per ward guidelines.

alerts.title: ALERT 4.1 – Equipment Disinfection
alerts.content: Follow manufacturer directions; low‑level disinfectants include 70‑90 % isopropyl alcohol, 0.05 % sodium hypochlorite, quaternary ammonium, or iodophor. Avoid phenolics in neonatal areas.

sections.title: Complications
sections.content: Both clinical and device‑related complications can occur with phototherapy.

subsections.title: CLINICAL COMPLICATIONS
subsections.content: Dehydration, hypothermia, retinal damage, eye infections, bronze‑baby syndrome, and acute bilirubin encephalopathy (kernicterus).

subsections.title: DEVICE COMPLICATIONS
subsections.content: Loss of therapeutic output after 20 000‑50 000 h of use; regular irradiance testing required.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
sections.content: Basic first‑line care to prolong equipment life.

subsections.title: POWER SOURCE
subsections.content: Units may be mains‑powered or battery‑backed depending on model.

subsections.title: WARD LOCATION
subsections.content: Typically rolling units with caster wheels; can be moved bedside as needed.

subsections.title: USER PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE
subsections.content: Test light output weekly with a phototherapy light meter (see ‘Setting up for a patient’).

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
sections.content: First‑line steps before contacting biomedical engineering.

subsections.title: The phototherapy light does not turn on
subsections.content: Check power cable, switch and outlet (7.1). If still non‑functional, contact maintenance.

subsections.title: The phototherapy light turns on, but only some lights are functional
subsections.content: Contact maintenance for bulb replacement. Verify you have switched on the blue therapeutic lights, not just the white examination lights (7.2).

title: Suction Machine (Pump) (E 1)
source: NEST360°. Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies‑Education – Clinical Modules: Suction Pump (June 2020)
learning_objectives: Recognise clinical situations where a suction pump is required for newborns and infants.
Select the appropriate suction device (pump, catheter, Yankauer or Penguin sucker) for a given patient size.
Demonstrate step‑by‑step set‑up, patient preparation, suctioning technique and safe removal of the device.
Apply infection‑prevention and disinfection procedures to minimise cross‑contamination.
Identify common complications (clinical and device‑related) and appropriate corrective actions.
Perform routine maintenance, weekly preventive checks and basic troubleshooting before calling technical support.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.summary: Obstruction of the nostrils, mouth or upper airway by secretions, blood or meconium can cause respiratory compromise and hypoxia. Suction pumps clear these obstructions. Routine suctioning is not recommended; only suction when obstruction is evident.
learning_objectives: Explain why suction is indicated only for airway obstruction.
Describe the risks of routine or unnecessary suctioning.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.summary: Overview of suction‑pump types, their components and pressure ranges. Highlights adult vs. paediatric/neonatal pumps, bacterial filters and reusable Penguin suckers.
learning_objectives: Identify the parts of a suction pump (pump, collection reservoir, filter, tubing).
Distinguish between adult, paediatric and neonatal suction pumps based on vacuum range.
Explain the purpose of the bacterial filter and why Penguin suckers are preferred over non‑autoclavable bulbs.

subsections.title: ALERT 2.1 – Suction Efficacy
subsections.content: Recommended suction pressure for neonates is 60‑100 mm Hg for ≤ 10 seconds (WHO range 50‑100 mm Hg).

sections.title: Management
sections.summary: Comprehensive guide to using a suction pump: set‑up, patient preparation, suctioning technique, care during the procedure and safe removal.
learning_objectives: Assemble all required components before contacting the patient.
Explain how to set vacuum levels safely for neonates.
Demonstrate correct depth measurement for suction catheters and safe insertion techniques.
Recognise signs of trauma or misplacement during suctioning and modify technique accordingly.
Perform a gentle, timed suction event and document completion.

subsections.title: SETTING UP FOR A PATIENT
steps: Gather suction pump, reservoir, filter, short & long tubing, catheter/Yankauer, water container.
Inspect and clean the collection reservoir if needed.
Attach filter to the reservoir port labelled “Vacuum”.
Connect short tubing to filter inlet and long tubing to the “Patient” outlet.
Plug in and power the pump.
Connect catheter/Yankauer to long tubing.
Adjust regulator to 60‑100 mm Hg and test with water.

subsections.title: PREPARING A PATIENT
steps: Explain procedure, risks and benefits to the guardian; perform hand‑wash and don gloves.
Visually inspect oral/nasopharyngeal cavities for secretions.
Choose catheter (Fr 6‑10) for thin secretions or Yankauer for thick secretions (never in newborn nares).
If using catheter, measure from nose to ear and mark half‑way back with tape.
Place infant in a neutral, comfortable position.

subsections.title: STARTING A PATIENT
steps: Gather marked catheter or Yankauer, pump with tubing in place and water container.
Re‑iterate purpose of the procedure to guardians, wash hands and don gloves.
Power on the pump.
Connect the suction device to the long tubing.
Set vacuum to 60‑100 mm Hg and test with water.
Insert catheter to tape‑marked depth (or Yankauer as far as visible).
If needed, lubricate nostrils with 1‑2 drops saline for Yankauer use.
Observe patient’s response, rinse catheter after each side, repeat until secretions cleared.

subsections.title: CARING FOR A PATIENT (During Suction)
points: If fresh blood appears, reduce insertion force – trauma may have occurred.
If stomach contents are suctioned, re‑measure depth – catheter may be in oesophagus.

subsections.title: REMOVING A PATIENT
steps: Gently withdraw the catheter/Yankauer.
Observe the infant for recovery and ensure oxygen is reinstated if needed.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
sections.summary: Routine cleaning and disinfection of the suction system is essential to avoid healthcare‑associated infections.
learning_objectives: Apply hand‑ hygiene before and after suctioning.
Use new or fully disinfected tubing and consumables for each patient.
Store consumables in a clean, dry area and avoid kinked tubing.

subsections.title: GENERAL INFECTION PREVENTION
steps: Clean hands with soap/alcohol before and after device use.
Ensure all patient‑related tubing and catheters are new or thoroughly cleaned.
Never reuse suction catheters.
Dry tubing hanging it away from contaminated surfaces.

subsections.title: ALERT 4.1 – Equipment Re‑processing
subsections.content: Uncleaned suction pumps pose a major infection risk. Refer to WHO technical specifications or Jhpiego manual for detailed re‑processing guidance.

subsections.title: DISINFACTION AFTER USE
steps: Disconnect and discard or disinfect catheter according to protocol.
Turn off and unplug pump; replace dirty filter (4.1).
Wipe pressure‑gauge controls with gauze soaked in 70 % alcohol.
Clean pump housing per ward surface‑disinfection policy.
Clean and disinfect collection reservoir and all tubing after each patient (4.2).

sections.title: Complications
sections.summary: Both clinical and device‑related complications can arise from inappropriate suctioning or poor handling of equipment.
learning_objectives: Identify signs of hypoxia, trauma, vomiting or vagal stimulation during suction.
Recognise device hazards such as unsecured pumps and take preventive measures.

items.name: Hypoxia
items.description: May occur if oxygen is stopped during suction; reinstate oxygen as soon as airway is clear.

items.name: Trauma
items.description: Excessive pressure (> 100 mm Hg) can damage mucosa; stop suction if bleeding appears.

items.name: Vomiting
items.description: Over‑deep insertion can trigger gag reflex and aspiration.

items.name: Vagal stimulation
items.description: Deep suction may cause apnoea or bradycardia.

items.name: Improper positioning
items.description: Unsecured pumps on walls or shelves may fall, causing injury.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
sections.summary: Basic first‑line maintenance to extend pump lifespan and ensure reliable performance.
learning_objectives: Identify power source options and battery‑care recommendations.
Locate the pump in a safe, accessible area.
Perform weekly 15‑minute run‑time test.

subsections.title: POWER SOURCE
subsections.content: Pumps may be mains‑powered, battery‑powered or manually operated (foot/hand). Battery‑powered units should stay on charger except when needed for use during power outages.

subsections.title: WARD LOCATION
subsections.content: Secure the pump where staff can easily view and adjust vacuum; keep consumables nearby for emergencies.

subsections.title: USER PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE
subsections.content: Run the pump for 15 minutes weekly to keep mechanical components functional.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
sections.summary: First‑line steps to diagnose common failures before calling technical support.
learning_objectives: Check power‑cable connection and wall socket when device will not turn on.
Inspect collection reservoir level and tubing integrity if suction stops.

subsections.title: Device does not turn on
steps: Verify power cable is firmly seated and socket is live (7.1).
If still non‑functional, contact maintenance.

subsections.title: Device stops suctioning
steps: Ensure collection reservoir is not full; empty if necessary (7.2).
Check tubing for leaks or disconnections.
If problem persists, request technical assistance.

references.citation: Helping babies breathe provider guide. American Academy of Pediatrics, 2016.
WHO technical specifications for neonatal resuscitation devices. World Health Organization, 2016.
Curless MS, Ruparelia CS, Thompson E, and Trexler PA, eds. Infection Prevention and Control: Reference Manual for Health Care Facilities with Limited Resources. Jhpiego, 2018.

title: E2 Pulse Oximeter
source: NEST360°. Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies‑Education – Clinical Modules: Pulse Oximeter (June 2020). License: CC BY‑NC‑SA 4.0.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Explain why pulse oximetry should be part of routine assessment for every infant on admission.
Identify clinical scenarios that require continuous pulse‑oximetry (oxygen therapy, CPAP, assisted ventilation).

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Describe the physiological principle behind pulse oximetry (red/infra‑red LEDs, photodetector, SpO₂ calculation).
Differentiate between normal and poor pulse‑oximetry waveforms.
Identify the three main types of pulse‑oximeter units (fixed, handheld, finger‑clip) and their appropriate patient groups.
State the target SpO₂ ranges for neonates on and off supplemental oxygen.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Demonstrate how to set‑up a pulse‑oximeter and its probe for a newborn.
Explain the preparation steps for a neonate before applying the probe.
Apply the correct technique for attaching adhesive‑wrap, rubber‑wrap and clip probes.
Monitor and record SpO₂ and heart‑rate values, ensuring they correlate with clinical status.
Remove the probe safely and perform basic disinfection of the probe and device.

subsections.title: SETTING UP FOR A PATIENT
learningObjectives: Follow hand‑washing and equipment‑gathering procedures.
Power‑on the device and verify a red light on the probe.

subsections.title: PREPARING A PATIENT
learningObjectives: Explain the importance of informed consent with the infant’s guardians.
Select an appropriate probe site (wrist/foot for wrap probe, finger/toe for clip probe).
Prepare the skin with alcohol and position the infant neutrally.

subsections.title: STARTING A PATIENT
learningObjectives: Apply an adhesive‑wrap probe correctly.
Apply a reusable rubber‑wrap probe correctly.
Attach a clip probe without causing tissue damage.

subsections.title: CARING FOR A PATIENT
learningObjectives: Allow the trace to stabilise before taking measurements.
Document SpO₂ and heart‑rate, recognising the accuracy limits of the device.
Inspect the probe site regularly for skin integrity.

subsections.title: REMOVING A PATIENT
learningObjectives: Remove adhesive, rubber‑wrap, or clip probes safely.
Disinfect the probe site and the probe itself according to the probe type.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Perform hand hygiene before and after any pulse‑oximeter use.
Identify correct cleaning, disinfection and storage practices for probes and the device.
Understand the rationale for low‑level disinfection solutions in neonatal settings.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Recognise clinical complications that can arise from inaccurate SpO₂ readings (mis‑diagnosis, pressure sores, etc.).
Identify device‑related complications (poor probe fit, patient movement, ambient light, alarm settings).

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Describe the power options (replaceable vs rechargeable batteries) and charging requirements.
Outline safe storage and ward‑location practices for the device.
Perform basic preventive maintenance checks before clinical use.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Apply a step‑by‑step approach to resolve the most common pulse‑oximeter problems (device not powering on, no trace, flashing red light, slow stabilisation).
Know when to escalate to biomedical/maintenance support.

title: E 3 Oxygen Concentrator
overallLearningObjectives: Explain the clinical situations in which an oxygen concentrator is indicated for neonates and young infants.
Describe the technical specifications, flow‑types and basic physiology of oxygen concentrators.
Demonstrate how to set‑up, operate, and safely disconnect an oxygen concentrator for a patient.
Identify infection‑prevention practices required before and after each use of the device.
Recognise common device‑related complications and the steps required to mitigate them.
Perform routine care, preventive maintenance and filter‑replacement procedures.
Apply systematic troubleshooting steps before contacting technical support.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Identify when an oxygen concentrator should be considered as the primary source of oxygen.
Distinguish between single‑patient use and shared‑patient configurations.
sections.summary: Oxygen concentrators can supply oxygen to one‑or‑two patients directly, or be used with flow splitters or CPAP devices to serve multiple patients. They are indicated for sick children with hypoxia (SpO₂ < 90 %).

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Recall the flow‑range (2‑10 L/min) and oxygen‑purity (85‑95.5 %) of typical concentrators.
Differentiate between intermittent/pulse flow and continuous flow.
Interpret the visual indicators that confirm proper device operation.
sections.summary: Concentrators deliver 2‑10 L/min of oxygen at 85‑95.5 % purity using two sieve beds. Two flow modes exist: intermittent/pulse (pulsed breaths) and continuous (steady flow). Images illustrate the device and flow‑type selector.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Prepare the patient and environment before connecting the concentrator.
Set the appropriate flow rate and verify device readiness.
Perform the “Bubble Test” to confirm oxygen delivery.
Safely remove the patient and power down the device after use.
sections.summary: Guidance on step‑by‑step set‑up, patient preparation, and verification of oxygen delivery. Includes power‑on, flow‑setting, humidification, nasal‑prong attachment, and flow verification.

subsections.title: Setting Up for a Patient
learningObjectives: Plug in and power the concentrator correctly.
Allow the machine to stabilise for 5 minutes before use.
Determine need for humidification (≥ 4 L/min).
Attach correctly sized nasal prongs and perform the Bubble Test.
steps: Plug power cable into wall and turn on concentrator (3.1a‑3.1b).
Set desired flow; wait 5 min for indicator light (3.2).
If flow > 4 L/min, connect a humidifier.
Attach nasal prongs to oxygen port or humidifier (3.3).
Perform Bubble Test by submerging prongs in water (3.4).

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Apply routine cleaning of the concentrator housing according to ward guidelines.
Disinfect flow‑meter controls with 70 % alcohol after each use.
Follow institutional protocol for tubing re‑use and device disinfection.
sections.summary: Cleaning of the device and its accessories is essential to avoid hospital‑acquired infections. Separate sections outline general surface cleaning and post‑use disinfection steps.

subsections.title: General Infection Prevention
subsections.content: Clean the concentrator housing following ward surface‑disinfection procedures.

subsections.title: Disinfection After Use
steps: Turn off and unplug the device.
Disinfect flow‑meter controls with gauze soaked in 70 % alcohol (4.1).
Repeat surface cleaning of housing and LEDs after each use.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Identify device‑related complications such as inadequate oxygen concentration.
Know when to increase monitoring frequency or contact maintenance.
sections.summary: Device complications focus on low oxygen output; if the indicator shows a problem, maintenance is required and patient monitoring should be intensified.

subsections.title: Device Complications
subsections.content: Inadequate oxygen concentration (5.1) requires maintenance or replacement of the concentrator.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Describe power‑source options and proper ward placement for the concentrator.
Perform weekly filter checks and bi‑weekly cleaning of gross‑particle filters.
Run the machine for at least 15 minutes weekly to keep sieve beds functional.
Document all preventive‑maintenance activities in a logbook.
sections.summary: Covers power options, optimal location, preventive maintenance of gross‑particle and bacterial filters, and routine run‑in to preserve sieve‑bed performance.

subsections.title: Power Source
subsections.content: Concentrators may be powered from mains (with voltage protector) or a rechargeable battery, depending on model.

subsections.title: Ward Location
subsections.content: Place the unit in a clean, dry, well‑ventilated area, 30‑35 cm from walls for adequate airflow.

subsections.title: User Preventive Maintenance
subsections.content: Two filters require regular care:

steps: Pull the filter gently from the back.
Swirl in cool, soapy water, rinse, and air‑dry completely.
Store as a spare until next cleaning.

filters.note: Do NOT wash; cleaning is performed by the maintenance department.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Use a systematic checklist to diagnose power‑on failures, lack of flow, and low‑oxygen alarms.
Know when to involve technical/maintenance staff.
sections.summary: Provides three primary troubleshooting scenarios with step‑by‑step actions and associated image references.

subsections.title: Device does not turn on
steps: Verify power cable is securely attached and socket is on.
Press the reset button (7.1) if still off.
Contact maintenance if the device remains non‑functional.

subsections.title: Device turns on but no flow is produced
steps: Connect a “Christmas tree” nozzle to the oxygen port (7.2).
Inspect port for debris; clean with gauze soaked in 70 % alcohol (7.3).
Escalate to maintenance if flow is still absent.

subsections.title: Low‑oxygen indicator/alarm sounds
steps: Check and replace gross‑particle and bacterial filters; run device 10 min.
Confirm set flow rates are within the device’s max specifications.
If alarm persists, reduce flow; if still present after > 50 % reduction, contact maintenance.

title: E4 Oxygen Cylinder
learningObjectives: Identify the clinical situations in which oxygen cylinders are used for neonates and young infants.
Describe the physical characteristics, sizing system and pressure specifications of oxygen cylinders.
Perform a proper assessment of oxygen needs, including flow‑meter selection and target SpO₂ ranges.
Set‑up an oxygen cylinder safely for a patient and know the steps to remove a patient from therapy.
Implement infection‑prevention and disinfection procedures for oxygen‑cylinder equipment.
Recognise common device‑related complications and the safety alerts that accompany them.
Apply routine care, storage and preventive maintenance practices for oxygen cylinders.
Troubleshoot basic flow‑related problems before contacting engineering support.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.description: Oxygen cylinders may be used to provide supplemental oxygen directly to hypoxic patients, to be shared between patients using a flow splitter, or used with devices such as CPAP.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.description: Explanation of hypoxia, cylinder construction, capacity sizing, pressure, flow‑meter precision, and target SpO₂ for neonates.

sections.title: Management
sections.description: Guidelines for using an oxygen cylinder in clinical practice, from set‑up to removal of the patient.

subsections.title: Setting up for a patient
steps: Clean hands with soap and water or 70 % alcohol before and after handling the patient or tubing.
Secure the cylinder upright to a wall or stable object.
Assemble the pressure regulator and flowmeter; connect using the pin‑index connector; ensure the flowmeter is vertical.
Open the on/off valve and regulator; read the pressure gauge.
Connect the oxygen‑delivery device and adjust the flowrate.
Determine if humidified flow is required ( > 4 L/min).
Perform a bubble test to confirm flow at the patient end.

subsections.title: Removing a patient
steps: Close the flowmeter on the cylinder once the patient no longer requires oxygen therapy.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
sections.description: Routine cleaning and disinfection procedures to avoid hospital‑acquired infections.

subsections.title: General infection prevention
steps: Wash hands with soap and water or 70 % alcohol before and after handling the patient or tubing.
Clean the cylinder housing according to ward guidelines or with soapy water.
Ensure the stop valve is tightly shut when not in use and during storage.

subsections.title: Disinfection after use
steps: Close the flowmeter; follow hospital protocol for tubing disinfection.
Clean the flowmeter, gauge and dials with 70 % alcohol after every use.

sections.title: Complications
sections.description: Potential hazards associated with high‑pressure oxygen cylinders and safety alerts.

items.title: Fire
items.detail: Oxygen supports combustion; never use grease or oil on cylinder components.

items.title: Pressurised gas
items.detail: Cylinders are filled to 137‑200 bar and must be chained securely to prevent projectile hazards.

items.title: Cylinder empty
items.detail: A partially open stop valve can cause slow depletion; ensure valve is fully closed when not in use.

items.title: Cylinder unstable
items.detail: Heavy cylinders must be upright and chained; instability can cause falls, injury, or damage to gauges.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
sections.description: First‑line care responsibilities, storage, and preventive maintenance for oxygen cylinders.

subsections.title: Power source
subsections.content: Not powered.

subsections.title: Ward location
subsections.content: Keep cylinders secured to walls with chains, store in ventilated, clean, dry areas away from oil, grease, heat or ignition sources. Use a secure trolley for transport.

subsections.title: User preventive maintenance
subsections.content: Open the flowmeter and allow the cylinder to release oxygen for 1 minute each week if not in use.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
sections.description: First‑line steps to resolve common flow problems before contacting engineering.

subsections.title: No flow is emitted from the oxygen cylinder
steps: Ensure flow‑meter knob and cylinder valve are open.
Check the pressure gauge – the cylinder may be empty; arrange refill.
If still no flow, close the stop valve and send the cylinder for maintenance; replace with a full cylinder.

subsections.title: The cylinder is making an audible hiss
steps: Listen for leaks; tighten any loose fittings between regulator and cylinder.
If the hiss persists, close the stop valve, send to maintenance and replace with a full cylinder.

title: E5 Oxygen Splitter
overview: A low‑flow oxygen splitter that allows a single oxygen source to deliver adjustable flows to multiple neonates or young infants. It is used mainly for breathing problems and must be set up, monitored, cleaned and maintained according to the guidelines below.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Explain when an oxygen flow splitter is indicated (multiple hypoxic patients from a single source).
Identify patient groups (neonates and young infants) that benefit from low‑flow delivery.
Relate the use of a flow splitter to the broader context of oxygen therapy.
sections.contentSummary: Flow splitters are used when oxygen from one source needs to be delivered to more than one hypoxic patient at low flows.

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Describe the contribution of hypoxia to morbidity and mortality in newborns.
List the specifications of a typical flow splitter (0.1–2 L/min per port, unchanged O₂ concentration).
Determine the appropriate flow rate for a neonate (0.5–1 L/min) and required SpO₂ target (90‑95 %).
Explain how to monitor oxygen therapy using pulse oximetry.
sections.contentSummary: Assessment covers the clinical impact of hypoxia, device capabilities, and the target SpO₂ range for newborns.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Demonstrate the correct set‑up of an oxygen flow splitter for a patient.
Adjust each port’s flow regulator independently and verify the set flow.
Label and number ports and tubing to avoid delivery errors.
Safely start, monitor, and discontinue oxygen delivery.
sections.contentSummary: Covers set‑up, patient preparation, ongoing care, and removal of the patient from the device.

subsections.title: Setting Up for a Patient
learningObjectives: Secure the splitter in an accessible location and ensure regulators are open.
Connect inlet and outlet tubing correctly.
Calculate the total required oxygen flow and add a safety margin.
Turn on the oxygen source and confirm flow‑meter beads pop up.
Individually adjust each port’s regulator while observing L/min at eye level.
Number ports and tubing to prevent mix‑ups.
steps: Secure splitter to avoid dislodgement (3.1).
Connect splitter tubing to oxygen source (3.2).
Assess total oxygen demand and add extra 1 L/min (3.3).
Turn on source; ensure flow‑meter beads appear.
Adjust each port’s regulator to required flow (3.4, 3.5).
Number ports and label tubing.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Perform hand hygiene before and after handling the splitter or patient tubing.
Clean the housing and ports using ward‑approved disinfectants (soap & water, then 70 % alcohol).
Follow the post‑use disinfection protocol after every patient encounter.
sections.contentSummary: Routine cleaning and disinfection are essential to prevent hospital‑acquired infections in newborn units.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Identify hazards related to device positioning (fall risk).
Recognise the problem of dependent flow regulation and ensure independent flow splitters are used.
Detect absent or inadequate flow at the patient interface and know when to invoke troubleshooting.
sections.contentSummary: Potential device‑related and patient‑related complications that could affect safety and efficacy.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Understand that the splitter is a non‑powered device and must be mounted securely at eye level.
Perform a weekly 15‑minute test where each flowmeter is opened to its maximum flow.
Maintain proper ward location (wall‑mounted, raised edge, numbered ports) to reduce accidental falls and mis‑delivery.
sections.contentSummary: First‑line care and preventive maintenance actions to prolong equipment lifespan.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Systematically check the oxygen source, connections, and tubing when no flow is observed.
Isolate blockage or leakage in a single port and apply appropriate cleaning.
Recognise when to contact maintenance/engineering after basic checks fail.
sections.contentSummary: Step‑by‑step guidance for the most common flow‑related problems.

title: E 6 Oxygen Therapy
description: Overview of oxygen therapy for newborns and young infants, including when to use it, how to deliver it, monitoring, infection‑prevention, troubleshooting and maintenance.
learningObjectives: Identify clinical situations in newborns that require supplemental oxygen.
Understand the target SpO₂ range (90‑95 %) and how to titrate oxygen safely.
Select the appropriate oxygen delivery device (cannula, mask, CPAP, splitter, concentrator, cylinder).
Perform a systematic patient assessment before, during and after oxygen therapy.
Apply infection‑prevention and re‑processing procedures for oxygen‑related equipment.
Recognise and manage common clinical and device complications of oxygen therapy.
Implement routine care, maintenance and troubleshooting for oxygen delivery systems.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.summary: Oxygen may be delivered directly, via a splitter, or in combination with devices such as CPAP. Indications include hypoxaemia (SpO₂ < 90 %) caused by a wide range of neonatal respiratory and systemic conditions.
learningObjectives: List the neonatal conditions that may require supplemental oxygen.
Explain why continuous SpO₂ monitoring is mandatory when delivering oxygen.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.summary: Assessment focuses on recognising hypoxia, measuring SpO₂, and selecting appropriate flow rates for the chosen delivery method.
learningObjectives: Differentiate flow‑rate requirements for resuscitation, nasal prongs, masks and CPAP.
Interpret ALERT 2.1 and ALERT 2.2 regarding resuscitation FiO₂ and safe SpO₂ targets.

subsections.title: Flow‑rate table
content: Resuscitation (bag‑mask): 10 L min⁻¹ (Alert 2.1)
Flow‑meter stand (splitter): 1‑2 L min⁻¹ per port
Nasal prongs: ≤ 2 L min⁻¹
Mask: 3‑10 L min⁻¹
CPAP: 1‑6 L min⁻¹

sections.title: Management
sections.summary: Covers preparation, initiation, ongoing care, and removal of oxygen therapy. Device‑specific guidance is referenced to separate modules.
learningObjectives: Perform the ABC assessment and decide whether CPAP or low‑flow oxygen is indicated.
Demonstrate correct set‑up of nasal prongs and secure them safely.
Monitor SpO₂ continuously and titrate flow to keep saturation within 90‑95 %.
Follow a step‑wise wean protocol and criteria for safe removal of oxygen.

subsections.title: Preparing a Patient
content: Assess airway, breathing (RR > 60 bpm, SpO₂ < 90 %, work of breathing), circulation (perfusion, capillary refill), seizures and temperature.
Consider bubble CPAP if available and indicated.

subsections.title: Starting a Patient
content: Gather appropriately sized nasal prongs, tape, cotton wool.
Verify tubing connection, set flow, confirm oxygen is exiting the prongs.
Insert prongs loosely, secure with tape, protect skin, label ports.

subsections.title: Caring for a Patient
content: Continuous pulse‑oximetry, titrate flow to keep SpO₂ 90‑95 %.
If > 2 L min⁻¹ is needed, switch to CPAP or mask (Alert 3.2).
Monitor vital signs, work of breathing and nostril patency.
Apply nasal saline drops every 4 h (or more often).

subsections.title: Removing a Patient
content: Reduce flow by 0.25 L min⁻¹, re‑check SpO₂ after 15 min; repeat until stable at 0.25 L min⁻¹.
If stable > 90‑95 % for 15 min, remove tape and prongs; re‑check after 15 min.
Re‑initiate oxygen if saturation falls < 90 % or clinical status deteriorates.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
sections.summary: Emphasises hand hygiene, use of new or properly cleaned tubing, drying and storage, and rapid re‑processing of reusable components.
learningObjectives: Apply hand‑hygiene before and after handling oxygen equipment.
Identify steps for cleaning, drying and storing nasal prongs and tubing.
Explain the importance of timely re‑processing of single‑use respiratory circuits (Alert 4.1).

subsections.title: General Infection Prevention
content: Wash or disinfect hands before/after contact.
Use new or thoroughly cleaned tubing; label cleaned items.
Hang tubing to dry, keep off the floor, store in a clean dry area.

subsections.title: Disinfection After Use
content: Detach tubing, start cleaning protocol immediately if re‑using.
If not re‑using, discard according to waste policy (Alert 4.1).
Replace humidifier water after each patient or daily.

subsections.title: Reprocessing Tubing
subsections.content: Refer to the illustrated algorithm (coin76.png) for step‑by‑step cleaning, disinfection and storage.

sections.title: Complications
sections.summary: Distinguishes clinical complications (hypoxia, hyperoxia, nasal blockage, necrotic septum) from device‑related complications (inadequate oxygen concentration).
learningObjectives: Recognise signs of hypoxia and hyperoxia and their potential sequelae.
Identify equipment‑related problems such as blocked prongs or poor FiO₂ delivery.
Implement preventive measures to avoid nasal tissue injury.

subsections.title: Clinical Complications
content: Hypoxia → brain injury, multi‑organ failure, death.
Hyperoxia → retinopathy of prematurity, chronic lung disease.
Nasal blockage, necrotic septum, displaced prongs.

subsections.title: Device Complications
content: Inadequate oxygen concentration from any delivery system can limit therapeutic effect.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
sections.summary: Power, location and preventive maintenance differ by device (concentrator, cylinder, splitter). Specific guidance is provided in the linked modules.
learningObjectives: Identify the power requirements for each oxygen‑delivery device.
Describe routine preventive‑maintenance tasks for concentrators, cylinders and splitters.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
sections.summary: Typical failures and repair steps vary by device type; see the dedicated modules for detailed procedures.
learningObjectives: Diagnose common faults in oxygen concentrators, cylinders and splitters.
Apply basic repair or replacement steps before seeking technical support.
