title: E 8 Radiant Warmer
learningObjectives: Recognise the clinical situations that require a neonatal radiant warmer.
Explain the four mechanisms of neonatal heat loss and how radiant warmers counteract them.
Select and set the appropriate warming mode (pre‑warm, automatic/servo, manual).
Demonstrate safe set‑up, patient preparation, placement, monitoring and removal of a newborn on a radiant warmer.
Apply infection‑prevention and cleaning protocols to maintain device safety.
Identify common clinical and device‑related complications and initiate appropriate corrective actions.
Troubleshoot basic power, heating and probe failures before contacting maintenance.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
learningObjectives: Understand why temperature < 36 °C at birth increases mortality risk.
Identify newborn groups for which radiant warming is indicated.

sections.title: Assessment
learningObjectives: Describe the four mechanisms of neonatal heat loss (evaporation, radiation, conduction, convection).
Know the normal axillary temperature range (36.5 °C‑37.5 °C).
Differentiate the three warming modes (pre‑warm, automatic/servo, manual).

subsections.title: Heat‑loss mechanisms & warming modes
subsections.content: Summarises evaporation, radiation, conduction and convection; explains how radiant warmers provide focused radiative heat and outlines pre‑warm, automatic/servo and manual settings.

sections.title: Management
learningObjectives: Set‑up the warmer safely (power, temperature probe, heating mode).
Prepare the newborn and required consumables before positioning.
Place the infant correctly and secure the temperature probe.
Monitor temperature and alarms according to the selected mode.
Remove the infant safely and disinfect the device.

subsections.title: Setting up for a patient
subsections.content: Plug power, select pre‑warm or 25 % manual, connect temperature probe and verify heat from the overhead elements.

subsections.title: Preparing a patient
subsections.content: Gather tape, gauze, 70 % alcohol; explain procedure to guardians; perform hand‑washing; ensure the baby is dry and wearing a hat.

subsections.title: Starting a patient
subsections.content: Pre‑warm the cot, switch to servo/automatic mode, position infant centrally, clean and place the temperature probe over the liver (or flank for prone babies) and secure it.

subsections.title: Caring for a patient
subsections.content: Monitor temperature 5 min after start then every 4 h (servo) or 30 min (manual); respond to temperature, probe, power and system alarms.

subsections.title: Removing a patient
subsections.content: Disconnect tape, disinfect probe site and probe, turn off and unplug the warmer, check infant’s temperature after 30 min, and disinfect the cot before reuse.

sections.title: Infection Prevention
learningObjectives: Apply hand‑ hygiene before and after patient contact.
Ensure all consumables (probes, tape, gauze) are clean or single‑use.
Follow post‑use disinfection procedures for probe, control panel, mattress and cot.

subsections.title: General infection prevention
subsections.content: Hand washing, use of new/clean consumables, proper storage of cables, and dedicated probe per infant.

subsections.title: Disinfection after use
subsections.content: Turn off and cool the unit, wipe probe, panel, power button, mattress and cot with gauze and 70 % alcohol or diluted chlorine.

sections.title: Complications
learningObjectives: Identify clinical complications (hypothermia, hyperthermia, pressure sores, falls, infection).
Recognise device‑related complications (probe mis‑management, alarm mis‑setting, fire risk).

subsections.title: Clinical complications
subsections.content: Discusses hypothermia, hyperthermia, pressure sores, falls and infection risks linked to improper use.

subsections.title: Device complications
subsections.content: Covers overheating from probe loss, improper alarm thresholds, and fire hazards from linen placement.

sections.title: Care & Maintenance
learningObjectives: Understand power requirements and the need for dedicated mains sockets.
Place the warmer correctly in the ward to minimise drafts.
Perform weekly preventive checks of heating elements, probe and power‑loss alarm.

subsections.title: Power source
subsections.content: Warmers use mains power; plug into a dedicated socket with surge protection; not suitable for low‑power backup systems.

subsections.title: Ward location
subsections.content: Place against a wall, control panel facing centre of room, keep away from windows and drafts.

subsections.title: User preventive maintenance
subsections.content: Weekly test of heating elements & probe by feeling heat and checking temperature rise; test power‑loss alarm by disconnecting mains.

sections.title: Troubleshooting & Repair
learningObjectives: Diagnose why the unit will not power on.
Identify reasons for lack of heat despite power.
Resolve temperature‑probe reading failures.

subsections.title: Problem 1 – Unit does not turn on
subsections.content: Check power cable connection, power switch; replace cable if needed; contact maintenance if still off.

subsections.title: Problem 2 – Unit powers on but does not heat
subsections.content: Verify heating setting is > 0 % (manual) or correct mode selected; replace cable if needed; contact maintenance if heating still absent.

subsections.title: Problem 3 – Probe not reading temperature
subsections.content: Hold probe in hand and watch for temperature change; replace probe if no change or alarm persists; contact maintenance.

title: E 9 Glucometer
learning_objectives: Describe why a glucometer is essential for detecting and managing neonatal hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia.
Identify the clinical signs and risk‑factors that should prompt blood‑glucose testing in newborns.
Perform a complete glucometer‑based blood‑glucose assessment, from preparation through result documentation.
Apply infection‑prevention and device‑cleaning practices to minimise cross‑contamination.
Recognise common clinical and device‑related complications of glucometer use and know how to respond.
Execute routine care, preventive maintenance and basic troubleshooting of a glucometer.

sections.title: Clinical Problem
sections.content: Assessment of blood glucose with a glucometer should be performed on routine admission for all infants and continued for all sick or at‑risk newborns.  Hypoglycaemia may present with jitteriness, irritability, hypotonia, lethargy, reduced consciousness, poor feeding, seizures, hypothermia, apnoea/irregular breathing, or it may be asymptomatic.  Prematurity, intra‑uterine growth restriction, birth‑asphyxia, infants of diabetic mothers and other sick babies are especially prone to hypoglycaemia.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.content: Hypoglycaemia occurs in ~10 % of healthy neonates and is the most common neonatal medical emergency.  Glucometers provide rapid, approximate whole‑blood glucose values; results should be confirmed by laboratory testing when persistent, recurrent, or accuracy is in doubt.  Test strips use a glucose‑oxidase electrode that generates a current proportional to glucose concentration.

subsections.title: SETTING UP FOR A PATIENT
steps: Collect glucometer, strips, and control solutions.
Turn on the glucometer (press power button or insert a strip).
Insert a test strip (click into place).
Perform a daily Quality‑Control test using control solution or known‑glucose solution and verify a ‘Pass’ result.

subsections.title: PREPARING A PATIENT
steps: Assess the infant for conditions associated with hypoglycaemia.
Explain purpose, risks and benefits to the guardians before proceeding.
Perform hand‑washing and don gloves.
Gather gloves, 70 % alcohol, cotton swab.
Clean the outer heel with alcohol‑soaked cotton; allow to dry.  Never use a finger puncture or a poorly perfused site.

subsections.title: ASSESSING A PATIENT
steps: Collect a new lancet, glucometer, strip, cotton swabs, alcohol, gloves and a tray.
Perform hand‑washing and don gloves.
Insert strip and turn on the meter.
Prick the disinfected outer heel with the lancet; obtain a blood drop (if none, massage heel).
Wipe away the first drop, then collect a second drop on the strip (device absorbs automatically).
Apply dry cotton pressure to stop bleeding.
Read the displayed glucose value, record it, and repeat if it conflicts with clinical status.
Compare result to normal thresholds (≤2.5 mmol/L = 45 mg/dL is considered hypoglycaemic).

subsections.title: CONCLUDING ASSESSMENT
steps: Remove and discard the used strip in hazardous‑waste.
Dispose of the lancet in a sharps container.
Remove gloves, discard, and perform hand‑washing.

subsections.title: GENERAL INFECTION PREVENTION
steps: Clean hands with soap/alcohol before and after any glucometer use; wear gloves throughout.
Thoroughly clean the infant’s skin before sampling; avoid sampling from infected or poorly perfused sites.
Use only new, single‑use consumables (lancets, strips, swabs, gloves).
Store strips in a dry, airtight container per hospital policy.
Follow universal precautions for sharps.

subsections.title: DISINFECTION AFTER USE
steps: Discard strip and lancet as above, remove gloves, wash hands.
Wipe the glucometer exterior with 70 % alcohol (avoid submerging or dripping into strip slot).

subsections.title: CLINICAL COMPLICATIONS
items: Artery, nerve or bone injury from inappropriate heel site.
Rare site infection if asepsis is inadequate.

subsections.title: DEVICE COMPLICATIONS
items: Falsely high readings due to dextrose gel or skin residues – cleanse and repeat.
Inaccurate results from expired or improperly stored strips – verify expiration and storage.

subsections.title: POWER SOURCE
subsections.content: Glucometers are powered by replaceable or rechargeable batteries (6.1).  Rechargeable units should be charged regularly when not in use.

subsections.title: WARD LOCATION
subsections.content: Store the glucometer and strips in a clean, dry, secure area.  If a docking/charging station is provided, keep the device on it when idle (6.2).

subsections.title: USER PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE
subsections.content: Besides battery care, perform a monthly Quality‑Control test using control solution or a known‑glucose solution (6.3‑6.5).  A ‘Pass’ confirms proper functioning.

issues.problem: The glucometer is not turning on
steps: Insert a test strip – many models power‑on automatically.
If still off, charge or replace the batteries.
If the problem persists, contact maintenance/engineering.

issues.problem: Results are consistently incompatible with the patient’s clinical condition
steps: Check expiration date of strips; replace if expired.
Run a Quality‑Control test (see Care & Maintenance ► Preventive Maintenance).
If discrepancy remains, seek technical support.

title: Equipment
learningObjectives: Understand the purpose and proper use of essential newborn and young‑infant equipment.
Identify each piece of equipment and describe its role in neonatal care.
Locate detailed reference pages for every equipment item listed.

sections.title: Foreword
sections.content:
Malawi is one of the countries that made significant progress in reducing child mortality, achieving MDG 4 targets ahead of the 2015 deadline recognised in the Promise Renewed report of 2014. The current under‑five mortality rate has fallen to 71 per 1,000 live births from 244 in 1999. Neonatal mortality is still very high at 27 per 1,000 live births and accounts for over 40 percent of under‑five mortality. Newborn survival is receiving international attention, with an established global target of 12 deaths per 1,000 live births by 2030. Newborns in Malawi continue to die from preventable conditions—infection, prematurity and asphyxia. Malawi’s Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP) targets reducing preventable neonatal deaths from 27 per 1,000 live births to 23 by 2020 and to 15 per 1,000 live births by 2035. Improving quality of care is one of the pillars for ensuring attainment of the objectives.

The Ministry of Health (MoH) fully recognizes the immediate and long‑term health, social and economic repercussions of acute and prolonged morbidity and mortality among neonates and infants. In the last few years there have been major advances for the care of newborns in Malawi. To achieve these ambitious targets purpose‑built special newborn care units (SNCU) have been opened in most hospitals. Nurses and midwives are now assigned to the neonatal wards and a package of equipment for comprehensive care has been provided to every hospital in Malawi. Trained and skilled health workers are one of the cornerstones of quality care and in order to address the knowledge and skills gap that were there, the MoH in collaboration with the Paediatric and Child Health Association (PACHA) and UNICEF developed the Care of the young Infant and Newborn (COIN) training course in 2015. The COIN manual and course have been updated on a regular basis to reflect new developments in neonatal care and respond to changes in practice.

The use of modern technologies played a vital role in improving outcomes of neonates in the West and is critical if Malawi is to achieve the ambitious neonatal mortality targets. To address this need the MOH partnered with NEST 360° (Rice University and Malawi College of Medicine) to create a package of durable, high‑impact, low‑cost technologies for comprehensive newborn care. For these reasons a supplement has been added to the COIN course to explain the use and maintenance of the technologies. The manual is intended to be used by clinicians, nurses/midwives and biomedical technicians/engineers involved in taking care of neonates and young infants with the equipment placed in the SNCUs. The manual contains guidelines on basic clinical management of major causes of morbidity and mortality of neonates and young infants presenting to health facilities in Malawi. It is complementary to the existing MoH policies and guidelines on Maternal, Newborn and Child Health.

It is the hope of the Ministry that this document will be widely disseminated and utilised across the country to achieve the desired goal. The MoH would like to acknowledge and appreciate contributions made by individuals and institutions in various forms.

Dr Charles Mwansambo
Secretary for Health
learningObjectives: Describe the current state of child and neonatal mortality in Malawi and the national targets for 2020, 2030 and 2035.
Identify the key components of Malawi’s Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP) and the role of quality‑of‑care improvement.
Explain the initiatives undertaken by the Ministry of Health, including the establishment of Special Newborn Care Units (SNCU) and the COIN training programme.
Summarise the partnership with NEST 360° and the purpose of the technology supplement added to the COIN course.
Recognise the intended audience for this manual and how it complements existing MoH policies on maternal, newborn and child health.

subsections.title: Progress in Child Mortality & Neonatal Challenges
subsections.content: Malawi reduced under‑five mortality from 244/1,000 in 1999 to 71/1,000 today. Neonatal mortality remains high (27/1,000) and accounts for >40 % of under‑five deaths. Global target: 12/1,000 by 2030.

subsections.title: National Targets & ENAP Goals
subsections.content: ENAP aims to lower preventable neonatal deaths to 23/1,000 by 2020 and 15/1,000 by 2035. Improving quality of care is a central pillar.

subsections.title: Health‑System Strengthening Initiatives
subsections.content: Established Special Newborn Care Units (SNCU) in most hospitals, assigned dedicated nurses/midwives, provided comprehensive equipment packages.

subsections.title: COIN Training Programme
subsections.content: Developed in 2015 by MoH, PACHA and UNICEF; regularly updated to reflect new neonatal care developments.

subsections.title: Technology Partnership – NEST 360°
subsections.content: Collaboration with Rice University and Malawi College of Medicine to deliver durable, low‑cost technologies; supplement added to COIN to cover use and maintenance.

subsections.title: Purpose & Audience of the Manual
subsections.content: Guidelines for clinicians, nurses/midwives, and biomedical technicians/engineers on clinical management and equipment use in SNCUs; complements existing MoH policies.

subsections.title: Acknowledgements & Vision
subsections.content: The Ministry hopes for wide dissemination and thanks contributors.

manualTitle: Care of the Young Infant and Newborn (COIN)

sections.title: Introduction to COIN
content: Most deaths in neonates and young infants can be prevented with low‑cost interventions. It has been estimated that up to half of all preventable neonatal deaths could be reduced with optimal treatment of neonatal illness.

In addition to providing care to newborns at birth, a district health facility receives sick young infants with diverse clinical presentations, some of whom are extremely sick and need emergency treatments. This manual and the course associated with it will deal with care of newborns at birth, the first few days of life, and sick young infants who are likely to be encountered in a secondary‑level health facility.

There is a lot of overlap between the clinical presentation and the management of conditions in the neonate and the young infant (defined as an infant less than two months of age). In this manual, when referring to both age groups we will discuss as the Neonate/Newborn and Young Infant (NYI). The young infant who requires resuscitation may be a neonate who is only a few days old. The approach to initial resuscitation is slightly different between the neonate and young infant, but the skills required are the same and you will practice these during this course.

This manual is for the candidate participating in a training on the Care of the Young Infant and Newborn (COIN). Lectures, videos, drills, and scenarios are the main methods used to deliver the course. The training is targeted at nurses, midwives, clinical officers, medical officers and medical assistants. The course is evidence‑based and strives to balance best practice with pragmatic solutions for our setting. Clear directions are provided for common clinical situations; if unclear, learners are encouraged to consult a colleague or a central hospital.

In the special newborn care unit, it is usual to have medical equipment not designed for hot, humid, dusty environments with an unstable power supply. Clinicians are often untrained on the use of these technologies and unsure of how to clean them or who to contact should the equipment fail. In addition to clinical knowledge and skills, the course equips participants on the management and use of technologies used in special newborn care units.
At the back of the manual there are wall charts or job‑aids that can be printed and placed in all clinical areas (clinic, ward, and special newborn care unit) where NYI are cared for.

On the last day of the COIN course, there is an examination that includes a Multiple Choice Questions (MCQ) paper to test knowledge and a competency‑based assessment that tests skills. Attitude (attendance and participation) is assessed throughout the course.

sections.title: Learning Objectives of COIN
sections.intro: COIN provides information that would allow an individual to be able to do the following:

learningObjectives.objective: Provide care at birth for all newborns
Provide care at birth for low‑birth‑weight infants
Provide neonatal resuscitation for those who need it
Provide emergency assessment and treatment for sick young infants (e.g., breathing difficulties, birth asphyxia)
Know how to use and maintain equipment necessary for the care of newborns
Understand which infants may benefit from referral and safe transport
Counsel families on common problems arising in this age group
Carry out an audit and introduce quality‑improvement activities in their own facility

subsections.title: Care at birth for all newborns
subsections.description: General routine newborn care immediately after delivery.

subsections.title: Care for low‑birth‑weight infants
subsections.description: Special considerations and interventions for infants of low birth weight.

subsections.title: Neonatal resuscitation
subsections.description: Initial steps and advanced techniques for newborn resuscitation.

subsections.title: Resuscitation for young infants
subsections.description: Resuscitation procedures appropriate for infants older than 28 days but younger than two months.

subsections.title: Emergency assessment & treatment
subsections.description: Rapid evaluation and management of sick young infants, focusing on breathing difficulties and birth asphyxia.

subsections.title: Equipment use and maintenance
subsections.description: Guidelines for safe operation, cleaning, and troubleshooting of newborn care equipment.

subsections.title: Referral and safe transport
subsections.description: Criteria for referral and principles of safe transport for critically ill infants.

subsections.title: Family counseling
subsections.description: Key messages and communication strategies for parents of newborns and young infants.

subsections.title: Audit and quality improvement
subsections.description: Steps to conduct an audit and implement quality‑improvement cycles in a health facility.

notes: The manual serves both as a participant guide for COIN training and as a bedside reference for NYI care.
Theory should be complemented with practical sessions during the course or through mentorship to achieve competency.
Successful completion of the COIN course requires passing both the MCQ (minimum 80%) and the practical scenario exam.

title: Wall Chart I – Algorithm: Emergency assessment and management of NYI airway and breathing

sections.heading: Emergency Assessment & Management of Newborn (NYI) Airway & Breathing
learningObjectives: Recognize the immediate signs of airway compromise in a newborn.
Perform a rapid, systematic airway assessment (positioning, suction, clear obstruction removal).
Initiate appropriate airway interventions (e.g., head‑tilt‑chin‑lift, jaw thrust, use of a laryngeal mask or endotracheal tube).
Evaluate breathing effectiveness (respiratory rate, chest movement, oxygen saturation).
Apply basic ventilation techniques when spontaneous breathing is inadequate (bag‑valve‑mask, CPAP).
Understand when to transition from basic to advanced resuscitative measures (chest compressions, medication).
Document the assessment and interventions accurately for hand‑over and quality‑improvement.

subsections.title: 1. Airway Assessment
points: Check for visible obstruction (e.g., secretions, meconium).
Position the head in neutral or slight extension.
Suction if needed – oral then nasal.

subsections.title: 2. Breathing Assessment
points: Observe chest rise and fall.
Count respiratory rate; note irregularities.
Use pulse oximetry if available.

subsections.title: 3. Airway Intervention
points: If obstruction persists – consider Magill forceps or laryngoscope.
Insert appropriate airway device (LMA, ET tube) if ventilation ineffective.

subsections.title: 4. Ventilation Support
points: Bag‑valve‑mask ventilation at 40–60 breaths/min.
Deliver 21–30 % oxygen initially; titrate based on SpO₂.
Consider CPAP if spontaneous breathing improves but is weak.

subsections.title: 5. Ongoing Re‑assessment
points: Re‑evaluate airway and breathing every 30 seconds.
Escalate to chest compressions if heart rate < 60 bpm despite adequate ventilation.
